Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents to facilitate further review of said transactions.
observations.managementAction:
No action has been taken by Management.

No action has been taken by Management.

Management submitted most of the lacking documents noted in audit.
observations.reason:
The BAC was not able to provide CNAS from PS-DBM or other proof of non-availability.

Several documentary requirements have not yet been submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Require the BAC to ensure that all technical and financial proposal forms are thoroughly reviewed to ensure that these are properly accomplished in accordance with the procurement law; and

Require the BAC to submit immediately to the Audit Team the lacking documents and those with deficiencies for further verification and technical evaluation.
observations.managementAction:
No action has been taken by Management.

Management submitted most of the lacking documents noted in audit.
observations.reason:
No action has been taken by Management.

Several documentary requirements have not yet been submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Information Officer, in coordination with the heads of departments concerned, to ensure that the Audit Team should be informed of the posting of the appropriate signboards within the prescribed period.

We also recommended that Management require the Municipal Engineer to ensure that the information on the tarpaulin signboards of infrastructure projects are provided and updated.
observations.managementAction:
No action has been taken by Management.

No action has been taken by Management.
observations.reason:
Management was not able to inform the Audit Team on the posting of signboards.

Review of pertinent documents showed no significant compliance with the audit recommendation.

observations.managementAction: The management has already traced a significant portion of the amount involved.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to: 
(a) recognize and record the differences noted in the Accumulated Depreciation as of December 31, 2023 totaling ₱1,111,446.32 in absolute value; and 

(b) thoroughly review the lapsing schedule and strictly follow the depreciation rules outlined in the notes to the financial statements to avoid inaccuracy.
observations.managementAction: The management already made the necessary adjustments.

observations.reason: Reconciliation of records is on-going

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management exhaust all remedies to validate the existence and propriety of the receivable accounts. Further, coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to facilitate completion of the documents required to make request for write-off of the dormant accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 for fair presentation of the accounts affected in the financial statements.
observations.reason: The management is still in the process of completing the documents necessary for write-off.

observations.recommendation: We, therefore, recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all his Department Heads in charge to observe the guidelines of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 in formulating PPAs to be funded out of the LDRRMF. Likewise, the MDRRMO must carefully plan the PPAs to be included in the LDRRMP and strive for their timely and full implementation and strictly utilize the funds according to the guidelines to achieve the maximum benefits for the Municipality’s constituents.
observations.managementAction: The management is conducting quarterly review on the utilization and implementation of PPAs of the LDRRMFIP.
observations.reason: For CY 2024, the management has optimally utilized its LDRRM Fund. However, prescribed forms were not strictly followed.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to:
strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts and financial reports in order not to delay the verification and post-audit activities; and
(b)observe the due dates of submission of the required financial reports and documents to the Audit Team by setting timelines of submission from Treasury Office to Accounting Office. Likewise, maintain processing and payment of transactions with complete supporting documents. Without, doing so, delays will affect both the Treasury Office and the Accounting Office on submission of accounts.
observations.reason: There is minimal improvement in the submission of accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), through its secretariat, to submit all contracts, together with all the supporting documents forming part thereof, within 5 days from its perfection to the Auditor to enable the latter to conduct auditorial review, before these contracts are forwarded to COA, Regional Office for technical evaluation.

In order to strengthen the internal control of the agency in safeguarding its assets, we highly recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to exercise the diligence of a good father of a family in supervising the Accountable Officers under his control to prevent the incurrence of loss or misuse of government funds and property or otherwise he shall be jointly and severally liable with them in pursuance to Sections 104, 123 and 124 of Presidential Decree (P.D.) No. 1445.
observations.reason: There are still unaccounted forms for CY 2024

observations.recommendation: We, therefore, recommended that the Municipal Treasurer account for the Seventeen Thousand Two Hundred Thirty-One (17,231) pieces of missing accountable forms and prepare/review in full the monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) before submission to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason:
Partial Report have been submitted. 
The Municipal Treasury Office is still on the process of verification of the subject – missing accountable forms.

observations.recommendation:
We, therefore, recommended that the GFPS:

conduct a gender analysis of their programs and projects through the administration of the HGDG tool to be able to incorporate the PPAs that address gender issues in the GPB as well as mainstreaming in the agency operations so that the formulation, implementation, evaluation, and accounting of results of the LGU’s annual GPB and GAD AR will be in order;

build capabilities through trainings to deepen their knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools among others; and

submit the DILG-reviewed GPB within the period required by Part V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014.
observations.reason: CY 2024 reports were not submitted within the due date.

observations.recommendation:
We, therefore, recommended that the MGSO:

regularly monitor the expiration and renewal of registration of all motor vehicles and apply or renew the registration of all serviceable motor vehicles of the Municipality with LTO and contracts of insurance with the GSIS to avoid penalties and payment of damages to third party liabilities in case of vehicular accidents; and

mark all Municipality-owned motor vehicles with the appropriate marking as required by Paragraph V.2 of COA Circular No. 75-6 dated November 7, 1975 to properly monitor the use hereof and gasoline expenditures. After which, furnish the Audit Team with pictures showing compliance with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Serviceable Vehicles are now properly labeled and insured. Pictures already submitted to the audit group.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that management conduct an investigation on the status of these NGOs/POs and various individuals and discuss with the appropriate committee in the Sangguniang Bayan any remedial actions that will be undertaken to the advantage of the Municipality.  

We further reiterated the following courses of action:

Strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 on the required analysis of dormant accounts by performing the regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors/accountable officers for strict monitoring and disposition of said accounts.

Determine the amount of the receivables impaired and provide reasonable allowance for impairment thereof in order to have fair presentation of the financial statements as required under IPSAS 29.

Comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 for the possible request for the write-off of the specified dormant receivables account to the Commission on Audit.
observations.reason: Management committed to complete the documents to facilitate the write-off of dormant accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal General Services Officer and the Municipal Accountant verify the existence of the inventories and prepare the necessary Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). Issue corresponding Inventory and Custodian Slip (ICS) for items being used and issued to concerned offices for monitoring, control and accountability.
observations.managementAction: Construction and Materials Inventory account was already adjusted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal General Services Officer and the Municipal Accountant verify the existence of the inventories and prepare the necessary Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). Issue corresponding Inventory and Custodian Slip (ICS) for items being used and issued to concerned offices for monitoring, control and accountability.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to direct the Municipal Accountant to determine the validity of the unpaid obligations or Accounts Payable totaling ₱340,785.88 which were outstanding for more than two years and if found to have no legitimate claim against the Municipality, cause the reversion of the same to the government equity.
observations.managementAction: Upon verification of all the long overdue outstanding accounts payable the Office of the Municipal Accountant will endorse these Payables to the Local Sanggunian for the adoption of Resolution for the possible reversion to the unappropriated surplus.
observations.reason: Per Municipal accountant, preparation is still on-going for the request of write-off.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant support the transaction noted in writing and adopt internal control measures to preclude the incurrence of unnecessary expenditures in the form of bank service charges to avoid disallowance in audit. Should the bank charges are found in order, the amount should be properly adjusted in the books of the LGU.

We recommended to the Management the following:
The Municipal Accountant to:

Ensure that the officers and employees with outstanding or unliquidated cash advances should not be granted additional cash advances, until the previous ones are settled, to preclude accumulation of unliquidated cash advances;

Ensure that all regulations pertaining to cash advances should be strictly enforced;

Monitor the outstanding cash advances of officials and employees and demand immediate settlement after its purpose has been served; and

Issue demand letter to settle the unliquidated cash advances.

The Municipal Treasurer to:

If warranted, withhold the salaries of those who persistently fail or refuse to comply despite repeated demand for liquidation and/or file appropriate administrative and criminal charges against them as instituted in the sanctions provided for under paragraphs 9.2 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 to compel liquidation.
observations.reason: Per Notes to FS, ₱35,000.00 represents unliquidated prior years cash advances granted to Emelson N. Jose for trainings and seminars. This employee was already separated from the Municipality.  Demand letters were already sent to the accountable offices.

observations.recommendation: (a) Local Chief Executive create an Inventory Committee to conduct an annual physical count of local road networks and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network not later than January 31 of each year; (b) requiring the Municipal Accountant to present in the Notes to Financial Statements the required disclosure for local road networks, and to prepare and maintain Local Road Network Ledger Cards for each local road; and (c) require the General Services Officer to prepare the annual Report of Local Road Network, and to prepare and maintain the Local Road Network Property Cards for each local road.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer stated that he was able to start the process of complying with the guidelines in the COA Circular, however due to difficulties in retrieval of old records the implemention was halted but will continue this year. The Local Chief Executive conducted audit findings and an Inventory Committee was created but inventory has not yet been completed.
observations.reason: This is really hard across the LGU and we need to really focus on this but we are trying our best. Lack of knowledge how to conduct proper physical count for local road networks and budget/time schedule differences among committees.

observations.recommendation: That the Management take full opportunity on the application of the prescribed guidelines and procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006, particularly the conduct of physical count of PPE, the recognition of PPE items found at station, and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items, if any, for the purpose of one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of the LGU and create and required the Inventory Committee to prepare the Physical Inventory Plan, to be approved by the Local Chief Executive and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period, and to comply with the prescribed unique property number indicated in the property stickers used for inventory tagging.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory and Disposal Committee will update all the PPEs at hand and have it approved by the Local Chief Executive and submit the said report to COA and Municipal Accountant so that it will reflect in the financial statements. However, due to limited manpower they could not fully undertake the one-time cleansing yet.
observations.reason: Lack of manpower; limited resources prevented undertaking the one-time cleansing despite intent and planning.

observations.recommendation: 1.6  We recommended that the Management require the OIC Municipal Assessor to prioritize and expedite all legal remedies to facilitate the immediate processing and transfer of ownership of lands under the name of the Municipality and to closely monitor the status of the transfers.

2.5   We recommended and the Management reinforce the compliance with VAT exemption provisions by requiring the following –
Preparation of ABC by end-user must be reviewed by personnel knowledgeable in taxation matters before approval;
Provide training and guidance to procurement and accounting personnel to ensure proper understanding and application of VAT exemptions; and
Coordinate with suppliers to secure refund for the VAT wrongly imposed on VAT exempt products, and to the Bureau of Internal Revenue on the options to recover or utilize the overpaid taxes.

3.4  We recommended that the Management require the LSB to ensure the submission of quarterly and annual reports on SEF Utilization to the DepEd CO and furnish the same to the ROs of DBM and DILG within the set timelines

We recommended that the Management instruct the MT to ensure the immediate and regular transmittal of paid vouchers/payroll to the MAO. Also, we recommend that the Management implement corrective measures by enhancing internal controls procedures and assigning personnel to monitor the timely flow of documents to avoid recurrence of delays and maintain accountability.

5.3  We recommended that the Management safeguard the interest of the Municipality and ensure compliance to procurement laws by requiring the Certificate of Final Acceptance before releasing the retention money. Any, premature release of retention must be properly reviewed to ensure that any discovered defects or third part claims are properly addressed by the contractors.

6.6   We recommended and the Management agreed to –
strengthen enforcement measures by issuing notice of all delinquency and publication, if warranted request the assistance of barangays for the release of notices;
request the Sangguniang Bayan for assistance in the enforcement of the remedies for the collection of delinquencies after the submission of certified list of all RPT delinquencies which remained uncollected or unpaid with statement of the reason for non-collection thereof; and
avail remedies by administrative action thru levy on real property or by judicial action.

1.5 We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to
avail of the one-time cleansing of dormant receivable accounts, file a
request for authority to write-off the identified dormant receivables,
supported with documents enumerated in Annex 8 of COA Circular No.
2023-008 dated August 17, 2023 in order to present fairly the receivables
and government equity accounts reflected in the financial statements.

2.8 We recommended that the LCE direct the Inventory Committee to
conduct physical count of its Property, Plant and Equipment and to
submit immediately the required RPCPPE to the AT not later than
January 31 of each year as mandated in the aforementioned Section 124,
Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs. Furthermore, we recommended that
Management avail of the advantages of COA Circular No. 2020-006
dated January 31, 2020 by strict compliance with its provisions. in order
to reflect more reliable and accurate balances of PPE accounts, hence to
convey precise information useful in decision making and accountability
for these properties.

3.4 We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Treasurer and
Municipal Accountant to reconcile the discrepancy of the cash balances
between the cashbooks and the ledger to reflect the correct amount of
Cash - Local Treasury account in the FS, furthermore, reconcile the
cashbook and ledger at least quarterly or as the need arises in order to
have an accurate, complete and reliable cash balance at any given time.

4.10 We recommended that the LCE require:
4.10.1 the Municipal Treasurer to closely monitor the cash in bank
balances by updating the cashbook regularly and balancing it
daily and stop relying on the figures provided by the bank as a
basis for the issuance of check payments. Moreover, require him
to refund the bank charge amounting to P152,000.00 as the
incurrence of the bank service fees was a result of his inability in
doing his duties and responsibilities;
4.10.2 the Municipal Accountant to prepare an accounting entry to
establish the receivable, pending settlement of the Municipal
Treasurer. Reconcile monthly the cash in bank subsidiary ledger
with the cashbook in order to easily determine possible
inadequacy of cash in bank balances; and
4.10.3 keep only idle funds in the HYSA, determine quarterly possible
projects and programs which require adequate funding and
transfer the funds from the HYSA to the current account,
otherwise, make an agreement with the depository bank for the
immediate transfer from the HYSA to the Current Account if the
latter needs additional amount for any checks presented for
payment.

5.8 We reiterate our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact daily or in the next
banking day all collections turned over to him including his collections in
order not to expose the public funds to a possible risk of loss due to theft,
misappropriation or misuse.

5.9 Likewise, secure approval from the Sanggunian Bayan to open a current
bank account with the Landbank Sta. Maria Branch, Sta. Maria, Ilocos
Sur, to have a more accessible depository bank, thus saving travel time
and expenses and alienating the question that the municipality is far from
their depository bank, Narvacan Branch, Narvacan, Ilocos Sur and thus
preventing/avoiding the problem of late deposit of collections.

5.10 Also, designate a Liquidating Officer to be in-charge of collecting the
daily collections of the Collectors and deposit the same and intact daily or
in the next banking day to the authorized depository bank of the
Municipality, hence the Municipal Treasurer would be handling only the
cash advances for salaries and wages of the Municipality’s employees.

6.10 We recommended that the LCE require:
6.10.1 the collectors to remit to the Municipal Treasurer all their
collections, regardless of the amount, at the end of every business
day by accomplishing the RCD and;
6.10.2 the Municipal Treasurer to closely monitor and supervise the
remittance of all the collectors, verify the RCD, check the RAAF
and make a physical count of all the unused accountable forms
under the custody of the collectors and tally these with the ending
balance reflected in the RAAF. Likewise, see to it that all the
official receipts previously given to the accountable officers were
consumed/used/issued and corresponding collections were
remitted before another booklet is issued to them.
6.10.3 transfer/relieve the erring collectors to department/section where
they are not allowed to collect to minimize, if not eradicate
repeated violations.

7.1.6 We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Budget Officer to include in the budget under Personnel Services the
salaries including the share of the government for the mandatory
contributions, allowances and benefits of the casual employees
and consider the same in the determination of PS limitation,
hence, budgeted correctly will likewise be disbursed properly under
the correct accounts.

7.2.5 We recommended that the LCE:

a. to require the Municipal Accountant to submit the aforesaid
lacking documents, otherwise a Notice of Suspension will be
issued and henceforth, see to it that payments to casual
employees for services rendered be supported with complete
documentary requirements as prescribed in COA Circular
No. 2012-001; and
b. to require the Personnel Officer before hiring casual
employees, to prepare a certification that their services could
not be provided by any of the permanent employees.

8.5 We recommended that the LCE oblige the Department Heads require the
submission of Daily Time Records, together with the Application for
Leave and Certificate of Leave Credits and Travel Orders if applicable,
certified correct by the employee concerned and approved by his
immediate supervisor before paying their salaries and wages, hence,
payment of the same is substantiated, thus, attendance and absences of
every employee is properly monitored in compliance with the
aforementioned regulations.

9.8 We strongly recommended that the Municipal government facilitate
availment of FINDES or the Financial Data Entry System of the
Landbank of the Philippines to reduce cash advances as a way of
strengthening internal control on cash management.

10.4 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Accountant, Municipal Engineer and the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC) to cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents
observed for all the transactions in Annex A for further evaluation of the
transactions and to avoid suspension in audit. Also, oblige the Municipal
Accountant to ensure that all claims are duly supported with the
required basic documents to ensure validity and propriety of the
transactions.

11.5 We recommended Management to stop the practice of granting CDWs
with clothing allowance, cash gifts and other benefits as there is no
employer-employee relationship between them and the local government
unit. Likewise, stop the practice of perfecting a MOA stipulating therein
benefits which can be granted and availed of only by permanent
employees or those with employer-employee relationship, consistent with
existing laws, rules and regulations, otherwise it will be disallowed in
audit.

12.5 We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Engineer to
strictly monitor the implementation of the infrastructure projects to
avoid the incurrence of delays. Moreover, in the event of delays, require
the Municipal Engineer to prepare a Statement of Work Accomplished
as of the target completion date as a basis for the computation of
liquidated damages and the Municipal Accountant to deduct the
computed amount from the claim of the contractor, thus to avoid
incurrence of government financial loss.

13.7 We recommended that the LCE require the BAC to conduct pre-bid
conference not earlier than seven calendar days from the date of posting
the Invitation to Bid as required under Section 22.2 of the 2016 IRR of
RA 9184 in order to give prospective bidders ample time to access the
PhilGEPS website and attend said conference to enable them to clarify
matters regarding the bid.

14.6 We recommended that the LCE require the BAC to:
14.6.1 upload the complete set of bidding documents in the PhilGEPS
and agency website so that all interested bidders can have equal
opportunity in accessing the needed information;
14.6.2 issue bidding documents only after payment of the required fee
unless uploaded in the PhilGEPS or agency websites, in which
case the bid proposals will be accepted only after the payment of
the required fee, thus impartiality in the conduct of the
procurement process will be observed.

15.5 We recommended that management see to it that all government projects
to be implemented are guided by Section 17.6 of the 2016 IRR of RA 9184
in case of pending acquisition of site or location to safeguard the
government funds from risks of undue donations, thus implement
projects only on government owned lots and lots that are free from any
future entanglements to avoid possible wastage of government funds.

16.8 The Audit Team recommended that the LCE:
16.8.1 require the concerned implementing offices the prompt
implementation of all projects/programs funded out of the
tobacco excise tax in order to give the constituents, especially the
tobacco farmers the early benefits that could be derived
therefrom;
16.8.2 advise the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator
(MPDC) and Municipal Accountant to determine the total
unexpended balances of the completed and unimplemented
projects and inform the LCE for possible reappropriation;
16.8.3 recommend to the Sangguniang Bayan to reappropriate for the
priority project the funds saved from the fully implemented and
the unimplemented projects for which implementation of the
latter are no longer viable hence, funds could be used in
accordance with the authorized uses as prescribed in RA Nos.
7171 and 8240.;
16.8.4 require the MPDC to include only feasible programs/projects/activities in the Annual Investment Plan and ensure implementation of the same; and

16.8.5 require the Municipal Engineer, MPDC, concerned implementing
departments, and the Programs/Projects Monitoring Committee
to monitor regularly the of implementation of all planned PPAs in
order to take appropriate actions in attaining all the targets in a
given period of time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant or any designated committee to recognize an allowance for doubtful accounts by estimating the portion of receivables that are unlikely to be collected by considering accounts that are now legally unenforceable due to the expiration of the prescriptive period and the history of voluntary payments of delinquent taxpayers. This evaluation should account for the age of the receivables, the presence or absence of collection actions, and any patterns of taxpayer compliance or non-payment over time.

We recommended that the Management:
Take due regard of the provisions of COA Circular 2012-003 on repairs particularly on the enumerations under Annexes C and D to avoid incurring unnecessary and excessive expenses.

Consider disposing the fully depreciated motor vehicle in accordance with the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties to avoid further deterioration and generate higher proceeds from sale.

Consider the purchase of new vehicle, subject to the availability of budget.

Pro-actively coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to explore more sustainable and cost-effective solutions by determining the possible source of fund and by conducting a cost-benefit analysis for the need of purchasing a new motor vehicle when repairs are no longer economically viable in order to reduce frequent high repair costs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LSB to:
 
The LSB to provide copies of the approved annual SEF Budget to the MA and MBO, copy furnished the audit team, within 7 days from its approval as their basis for disbursement or certification of availability of funds & for recording purposes.


Submit quarterly and annual SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1 to the DepEd CO, Sangguniang Bayan of Burgos, La Union, and the Regional Offices of DBM and DILG not later than the 20th day after the end of the quarter & not later than February 15 of the next year.

Ensure that the SEF Utilization Reports are posted in at least 3 conspicuous places within the prescribed timeline to further the transparency and accountability in the allocation/utilization of the SEF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the ME, direct the Project Engineers to:

Strictly require all contractors to ensure their entire workforce is wearing the required safety gears or personal protective equipment at all times within the construction project site for the protection and welfare of both the construction workers and the general public.


 Closely monitor the proper implementation of CHSP & applicable standards by contractors at project sites by maintaining detailed records of contractor's non-compliance per site visitation, which can be referenced during future payment processes.


We recommended that MM and ME to formulate an internal policy that clearly defines the applicable sanctions/penalties for contractors found to be in the violation of the CSHP, in order to better ensure accountability and reinforce adherence to safety protocols.

We recommended that the MM direct the ME to:

Strictly enforce compliance with project specifications, particularly the proper installation of COA-prescribed signboards with frames.


Require contractors to submit photo documentation of completed installations.


Conduct regular and timely site inspections to verify that work conforms with the approved POW and its Detailed Cost Estimates.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to analyze the transactions affecting the balances of Continuing Appropriations and to make the necessary adjustments to correct the discrepancy between the ending balances in CY 2023 and forwarded balances in CY 2024 in the net amount of ₱40,909.89.

We also recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to provide the required disclosures on the face of the SCBAA and/or in the NTFS for the changes between the original and final budget and material differences between the budgeted and actual amounts to enhance transparency of the financial statements of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Cause the collection of the deficient business taxes totaling ₱98,407.72 and the corresponding penalties and interests from the concerned contractors; and
Examine the accuracy of the declared gross receipts of taxpayers by considering all available evidence to ensure that tax assessment is accurate and fair.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the payment of hazard pay on days when the eligible PSWs are not exposed to occupational hazards defined by the rules and regulations or do not meet the minimum criteria for eligibility;
Establish clear guidelines for determining qualified personnel, ensuring that only those who meet the criteria receive hazard pay; and
Instruct the HRMO and the Municipal Accountant to thoroughly review the submitted justifications and create a verification process to validate claims for proper hazard pay disbursement to eligible PSWs.

We recommended that Management instruct the HRMO to:
Require officials and employees to submit complete documentary requirements before processing requests for special monetization of leave credits; and
Ensure that all requests are supported by valid justifications and duly approved by the Head of the Agency.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Engineer to regularly submit a monthly list of all issued Building Permits and Occupancy Permits to the Municipal Assessor which shall serve as reference for assessment and timely updating of the Assessment Roll;
Direct the Municipal Assessor to conduct tax mapping and verify whether real properties within the Municipality are properly listed in the assessment rolls specifically those newly constructed or renovated structures; and
Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and the HRMO to evaluate the financial capacity of the Municipality to create and fund additional plantilla positions under the Municipal Assessor’s Office to enhance their ability to conduct regular tax mapping and fulfill its mandate effectively.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Engineer to strictly monitor the compliance of contractors with the CSHP during the implementation of government projects.  Specifically, we further recommended that Management direct the Municipal Engineer to ensure the actual presence of the safety officer and first aider throughout the project duration, as stipulated in the DOLE-approved CSHP to enhance safety, promote efficiency and ensure adherence to applicable laws, rules, and regulations.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to:
Continue issuing notices to delinquent taxpayers to remind and prompt them to settle their obligations;
Intensify information campaigns to raise public awareness on the importance of paying real property taxes, emphasizing the benefits of early or timely payment for both the taxpayers and the community; and
Fully enforce available legal remedies provided under the Local Government Code, including the levy and public auction of delinquent properties, to ensure the effective collection of long-outstanding tax dues.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Engineer to adopt geo-tagging applications for all civil works and infrastructure projects to ensure that geographic coordinates and locations are properly recorded, leading to more transparent, accurate and reliable reports of project accomplishments.  It will also help prevent project duplication and overlapping of projects.  Geo-tagged photos should be attached to the disbursement vouchers as part of the supporting documents.

We recommended that Management direct the Inspection Officer and GSO to submit fully accomplished IARs to the Office of the Auditor within 24 hours after the inspection and acceptance of all consumable deliveries by concerned agency officials.

We also recommended that Management instruct the Inspection Committee to sign all delivery receipts to confirm that all listed items are present at the time of inspection. This will enable the Audit Team to conduct timely inspections, identify deviations from specifications and recommend corrective actions, thereby mitigating the risk of accepting defective goods.

We recommended that Management direct the BAC to refrain from recommending the award of contracts to bidders whose registered line of business does not align with the goods to be procured.

We also recommended that Management instruct the BAC and TWG to conduct a more thorough post-qualification process by carefully reviewing the relevant permits and licenses required for the goods or services to be delivered or performed.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct all Job Order workers, whose annual income falls below ₱250,000.00, to accomplish and submit the Income Payee’s Sworn Declaration of Gross Receipts and pay the required Annual Registration Fee.  Proofs of compliance shall be submitted to the Municipal HRMO for proper documentation and monitoring;
Require the authorized representative of the agency to accomplish the Income Payor/Withholding Agent’s Sworn Declaration along with the list of payees who shall not be subjected to withholding taxes and submit them to the BIR upon receipt of the duly accomplished sworn declarations from the individual service providers; and
Require the Municipal HRMO to strictly monitor and enforce compliance with the annual filing deadlines.  Specifically, individual service providers shall submit the required sworn declarations and proof of registration on or before January 15 of each year, or prior to the initial income payment, whichever comes first.  Correspondingly, the agency’s documentary requirements, as the designated withholding agent, shall be submitted to the appropriate BIR on or before January 31 of each year, or by the 15th day of the month following the submission of a sworn declaration by a new income recipient.

We, therefore, recommended that the:
Municipal Accountant recognize and record the differences noted in the Accumulated Depreciation as of December 31, 2024 totaling ₱60,934.96;
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer improve coordination and establish a reporting mechanism on the status of ongoing and completed projects to prevent delays their recognition to the appropriate accounts and to recognize the same even in the event of non-availability of actual billings from contractors; and
Exert more effort to trace and verify the status and history of the remaining Construction in Progress accounts included in the beginning balances and take the appropriate action if verified completed.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant take up the necessary adjusting journal entries in the books of accounts to reflect the actual insurance expenses incurred and recognize the unexpired balances under the Prepaid Insurance and Other Prepayments accounts for CY 2024.
Henceforth, faithfully comply with the provisions of IPSAS regarding the application of accrual basis of accounting and strictly observe the proper use of accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We, therefore, reiterated our recommendation that Management exhaust all remedies to validate the existence and legitimacy of the receivable accounts. Further, coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the completion of the documents required to make request for write-off of the dormant accounts in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 for fair presentation of the accounts affected in the financial statements.

We, therefore, recommended that the management ensure full compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022 as supplemented by COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024. Specifically, classify all purchased and/or issued semi-expendables to their appropriate accounts and prepare and maintain all applicable prescribed forms, reports, and registries.

We, therefore, recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately account for the Five Thousand Four Hundred Ninety-Eight (5,498) pieces of missing accountable forms and review in full the monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) before submission to the Office of the Auditor.
Likewise, we highly recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to exercise the diligence of a good father of a family in monitoring the movement of the accountable forms and the collections and deposits of cash to strengthen the internal control of the agency in safeguarding its assets and to prevent the incurrence of loss or misuse of government funds and property.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive institute and adopt additional system of internal control in safeguarding the LGU’s assets, particularly Cash, and direct the Accounting and Municipal to process the payments of wages and various personnel benefits directly to the Municipal officials and employees through their bank’s ATMs.

We recommended that:
The Municipal Treasurer immediately liquidate the cash advance on the National Seal of Good Local Governance and submit the same to the Audit team; and
The management, under the leadership of the LCE, ensure a strict process on the liquidation of cash advances for timely submission and proper accountability. This includes enforcing clear deadlines, requiring the settlement of previous cash advances before issuing new ones, and implementing penalties for non-compliance. Regular monitoring and reminders should also be conducted by the accounting office to prevent delays following the rules set forth under COA Circular No. 97-002.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Ensure that only the unexpended balances of the Quick Response Fund and the DRRMF –MOOE are transferred to the STF at the end of the year, while those appropriated for Capital Outlays shall be made continuing in the General Fund until the projects funded thereof are completed and any savings shall be made available for use for future disaster risk reduction and management activities as provided in the LDRRMFIP; and
Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to ensure fair presentation of the balances of the affected accounts as presented in the year-end financial statements.

We, therefore, recommended that the MGSO regularly monitor the expiration and renewal of registration of all motor vehicles and apply or renew the registration of all serviceable motor vehicles of the Municipality with LTO and contracts of insurance with the GSIS to avoid additional expenses in terms of penalties and payment of damages to third party liabilities in case of vehicular accidents.

We, therefore, recommended that the GFPS submit the DILG-reviewed GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and GAD Accomplishment Report (AR) within the period required by Part V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), through its secretariat, submit all contracts, together with all the supporting documents forming part thereof, within five (5) days from its perfection to the Auditor to enable the latter to conduct auditorial review, before these contracts are forwarded to COA Regional Office for technical evaluation without prejudice to the penal clause cited under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.
In addition, we also recommended that Management assess the workload of the personnel in-charge of the submission of contract documents, ensuring greater efficiency in the delivery of public services. This assessment should also help determine if there is a need for additional personnel to support this function.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to:
Strictly comply with the regulation on the rendition of accounts and financial reports in order not to delay the verification and post-audit activities; and
Observe the due dates of submission of the required financial reports and documents to the Audit Team by setting timelines of submission from Treasury Office to Accounting Office. Likewise, maintain processing and payment of transactions with complete supporting documents. Without doing so, delays will affect both the Treasury Office and the Accounting Office on submission of accounts.

We recommended that management present photographs during the on- going construction showing that the RCPC were actually installed otherwise, the equivalent amount of the unperformed work item in the program of work shall be disallowed in audit in compliance with Section 62.2.2 of the procurement law.
Also, management is recommended to observe the provisions of Section 37.4.1 of R.A. No. 9184 as to the effectivity of the Notice to Proceed in order to provide the correct dates of contract implementation.

We recommended that management require the contractor to conduct necessary repair and remedial measures to address the defects pursuant to Section 62.2.2 of the procurement law.
Also, management is recommended to observe the provisions of Section 37.4.1 of R.A. No. 9184 as to the effectivity of the Notice to Proceed in order to provide the correct dates of contract implementation.

We recommended that management require the contractor to conduct necessary repair and remedial measures to address the defects pursuant to Section 62.2.2 of the procurement law.
Further, reconcile the inconsistency of project’s contract duration otherwise, the 10 days delay will be computed as basis for the liquidated damages to be imposed even the project has already been paid in full.
Also, management is recommended to observe the provisions of Section 37.4.1 of R.A. No. 9184 as to the effectivity of the Notice to Proceed in order to provide the correct dates of contract implementation.
We recommended that the Municipal Engineer inform the contractor to carry out rectification of the defects to maintain the durability of the concrete pavement.

We recommended that management observe the provisions of Section 37.4.1 of R.A. 9184 as to the effectivity of the Notice to Proceed in order to provide the correct dates of contract implementation.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant thoroughly review the amounts indicated in the DV prior to payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer submit Statement of Work Accomplished duly validated by his Office at the revised expiry date to support the liquidated damages to be imposed in accordance with the inspection report of the Technical Audit Group.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer support the remaining 30-day time suspension/extension and submit Statement of Work Accomplished duly validated by his Office at the revised expiry date to support the liquidated damages imposed in accordance with the inspection report of the Technical Audit Group.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer submit Statement of Work Accomplished duly validated by his Office at the revised expiry date to support the liquidated damages imposed in accordance with the inspection report of the Technical Audit Group.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue her effort in complying the preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements for fair presentation of Cash in Bank accounts of the Municipality.

1.19 We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor order the:
a) GSO-Designate to maintain Property Cards and update records regarding
acquisition, description, custody, disposal, and other information about the PPE, based on the source documents of the transactions, and reconcile with the Accounting Records and RPCPPE.

b) Municipal Accountant to update the record regarding acquisition, description, custody, estimated life, rate of depreciation, disposal, and other information of PPE in the PPELCs and ensure that the total balance of PPELCs tally with the balances of controlling PPE accounts in the General Ledger and the Financial Statements.

c) Inventory Committee in coordination with the GSO and Municipal Accountant:
    i. Prepare a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) to complete the physical inventory within the prescribed period for the one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts.
    ii. Prepare a report on the result of the physical count through RPCPPE including the list of unserviceable PPEs, missing PPEs, and items found at station.
    iii. Any discrepancy between the RPCPPE, PPELCs, and PCs shall be investigated, verified, and adjusted. The total reconciled balances for each PPE account shall be established as the correct balance of PPE at year-end; and
    iv. to undertake the disposition of the non-existing and unserviceable PPEs in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006.

2.21 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the department heads'
compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2015-008.

2.22 We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer together with the
Municipal Assessor:
a. Trace and assess every component of the Road Networks such as road
lots, road pavement, drainage and slope protection structures, and
other miscellaneous structures and submit the report to the Municipal
Accountant and the General Services Officer.
b. Assess the local road at every reporting date, whether there is any
indication that a component of the local road may be impaired.

2.23 We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate:
a. Segregate and recognize the cost of each component of the local roads
with identified cost as per report of the Municipal Engineer and
Municipal Assessor.
b. Prepare and maintain Road Network Subsidiary Ledgers and separate
subsidiary ledgers with complete description for each road and its
components, such as road lots, road pavement, drainage and slope
protection structures, and other miscellaneous structures and
reconcile with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map to ensure
completeness.
c. Prepare and maintain Local Road Network Ledger Card, Annex D of
COA Circular 2015-008.
d. Prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation
tor each component at the end of the year over the prescribed useful
life and no residual value shall be provided for the depreciable
components of the road network system.
e. Prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation for
each component at the end of the year over the prescribed useful life.
f. Provide disclosures on the total network system in the Notes to
Financial Statements (Annex B of the Circular).

2.24 Finally, we recommended that the General Services Officer-Designate to:
a. Render a report on Local Road Network - Annex A of the Circular, at
the end of the accounting period;
b. Maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map; and
c. Keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card tor all roads and
its components, (Annex E) of the Circular.

3.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant-Designate:
    i. immediately submit the lacking disbursement vouchers with its
    complete supporting documents to the COA Audit Team;
    ii. prepare the appropriate journal entry voucher to transfer the cost of
    completed projects from the Construction in Progress account to its
    proper Property, Plant and Equipment account, and to correct the
    recorded cost of materials distributed to recipient barangays.
b. General Services Officer-Designate:
    i. issue the inventories for distribution to the proper end-user, and have
    them sign in the Requisition and Issue Slip;
    ii. summarize the issued Requisition and Issue Slip for the month in the
    Report of Supplies and Materials Issued; and
    iii. submit to the Municipal Accountant the monthly Report of Supplies
    and Materials Issued, duly supported with the Requisition and Issue
    Slips, as basis for the recording of expense or construction-in-progress,
    as appropriate.

4.16 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant-
Designate:
a. Identify and trace all unremitted withholding taxes and coordinate with
the Municipal Treasurer for its immediate remittance to the Bureau of
Internal Revenue (BIR).
b. Strictly enforce the withholding of taxes at the time the income payment
is made, becomes payable, or is accrued/recorded as an expense or asset
in the books of the payor—whichever occurs first—in accordance with
applicable tax laws and regulations. This includes payments made for
internet services and for publication, except for transactions expressly
exempted by law.

5.17 We recommended the Municipal Mayor to order the Municipal Treasurer for
the immediate remittance of the GSIS Contributions and Loan Amortizations
based on the reconciled data of the Municipal Accountant-Designate.

5.18 We likewise recommended that the Human Resource and Management
Officer:
    a. reconcile records with the GSIS and promptly update any changes in
    employment status and salary adjustments in the GSIS Web MPS and in
    the prescribed GSIS format to ensure timely and accurate record
    updates.
    b. monitor the approval of employees' loan applications and immediately
    inform the person-in-charge of the payroll and Municipal Accountant to
    ensure timely deductions of loan repayments from the payroll.

5.19 Also, we recommended that the Office-in-charge of the preparation of payroll
to ensure that all premium contributions and loan amortizations are deducted
from employees' salaries in the payroll for the month they are due.

5.20 Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate:
    a. provide the Audit Team with an explanation of the discrepancies identified
    in the GSIS billing, the amounts withheld for contributions and loan
    deductions, and the corresponding remittances; and
    b. henceforth, conduct monthly reconciliations of employee premium
    contributions and loan amortizations by comparing GSIS billings with
    actual payroll deductions and remitted amounts. This practice ensures
    billing accuracy, facilitates timely remittances to GSIS, and guarantees
    correctness of deductions from the salaries of employees.

6.16 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor order the reconciliation on the
Pag-IBIG Contributions and determine the correct amount of contributions to
be remitted in CY 2024, and the immediate remittance of the amounts
withheld from employees and the government share for Pag-IBIG
Contributions, including the deficiencies.

6.17 We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the
records in the Pag-IBIG Fund are updated and the amount to be withheld in
the payroll shall reconcile with the amount to be remitted as per the system of
Pag-IBIG Fund.

7.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the employee in-charge of
the remittance, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant-Designate, to
thoroughly account for the balances of the "Due to PhilHealth" account. Any
necessary adjustments or corrections should be properly recorded, and all
unremitted contributions must be promptly remitted.

7.14 Additionally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate
reconcile records with the PhilHealth and ensure timely updates of employees'
employment status and salary adjustments in the Electronic Premium
Remittance System (EPRS). The monthly PhilHealth contributions reflected
in the EPRS should consistently match the amounts withheld and the LGU’s
counterpart contribution.

7.15 Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant-Designate conduct a
monthly reconciliation of PhilHealth contributions against the Preliminary
Employees Premium Remittance List before processing the Disbursement
Voucher for remittance.

8.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor take proactive measures to
ensure that all insurable LGU properties are covered under the General
Insurance Fund (GIF) of the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS),
in compliance with R.A. No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular
2018-002 to mitigate the risk of losses resulting from calamities and unforeseen
events. Also designate a person in-charge to:

a. Prepare the Property Inventory Form and submit to the Supervising
Auditor/ Audit Team Leader and the GSIS not later than April 30 of each
year; and

b. Determine the latest appraised value of all insurable properties. An in-
house appraisal shall be sufficient if the property or insurable interest has
a value of P10 million and below. Otherwise, an independent appraisal
shall be necessary.

9.17 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as Chairperson of the Municipal
DRRM Council (MDRRMC) and the MDRRMO ensure that disbursements
charged under the fund align with eligible disaster preparedness, response,
and recovery efforts as enumerated per thematic areas of DRRM under the
NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March
25, 2013.

9.18 We likewise recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer:
    a. Strictly adhere to the approved annual budget and ensure that all
    expenditures are supported with available appropriation;
    b. Monitor the budget and augmentation which shall only be from savings
    in other items within the same expense class of their respective
    appropriations; and
    c. Monitor the changes in the annual budget based on the approved
    Supplemental Budget and any budget realignments approved by the
    Sangguniang Bayan (SB), or by the Municipal Mayor as authorized by
    the SB, through an Ordinance.

9.19 We finally recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the
MDRRMO submit justification on the disbursements made in excess of the
appropriations.

9.20 This is without prejudice to the issuance of a Notice of Disallowance.

10.18 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Point
System-TWG to:

a. Undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues
and ensure that the programs, projects and activities are responsive to the
said issues.

b. Prepare a GAD Agenda identifying priority gender issues based on the
GAD-related laws and policies, results of gender analysis and/or gender
assessment, and results of program and project evaluation in terms of
benefits to target beneficiaries.

c. Formulate GAD Plan and Budget based on the GAD Agenda and in
accordance with the latest guidelines prescribed under PCW-DILG-DBM-
NEDA JMC No. 2024-01, dated December 2, 2024.

d. Enhance knowledge and skills in gender mainstreaming, gender analysis,
gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools
through participation in relevant GAD training programs.

11.18 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant-
Designate to:

i. strictly observe compliance with the provisions of Executive Order No.
77 on the rates and apportionment of daily travel expenses, in the absence
of internal guidelines of the Municipality or an agreed-upon rates and
terms in the contract of Job Orders; and
ii. ensure that travel claims of Job Order personnel are duly supported with
complete documents which shall include the Certification of Expenses
Not Requiring Receipts, and the additional Certification from the Head
of the Agency or any authorized approving official as required in Item 5
of the Specific Guidelines per COA Resolution No. 2021-044.

12.12 We recommended that the Local School Board furnish copies of the approved
SEF Budget to the Local Budget Officer, the Local Accountant, and the Local
Treasurer within seven (7) days from its approval. Furthermore, the quarterly
and annual reports on SEF Utilization, prepared by the Municipal Accountant,
should be submitted to the DepEd Central Office, copy furnished the
concerned local sanggunian and the Regional Office of the Department of
Budget and Management (DBM), not later than the 20th day after the end of
each quarter for quarterly reports, and not later than the 15th day of February
for annual reports.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the adjusting
entries to correct the misclassified asset accounts with its corresponding
accumulated depreciation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the adjusting
journal entry to reverse the recognition of the BFP Building and
derecognize the donated portion of the lot amounting to ₱7,128,783.93
and ₱305,000.00, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record expenses in
their appropriate accounts to avoid the presentation of misleading
information in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the:
4.9.1. Municipal Engineer certifies that the Old Public Market was totally
damaged by Typhoon Odette and that the Old Municipal Gym was
demolished duly noted by the Local Chief Executive;
4.9.2. Municipal Accountant:
a. Prepare the adjusting entries in the books of the accounts
supported by the certifications issued by the Municipal
Engineer per recommendation no. 1; and
b. Coordinate with the Municipal Engineer in conducting a
thorough reconciliation of the reported damages in all other
assets involving civil works and the corresponding
derecognition to ensure that costs are properly reflected in
the books.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare the
adjusting entries to correct the affected accounts presented in the
Financial Statements and record on-going projects in the appropriate
accounts.

We recommended that the:
6.9.1. Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant reconcile the
differences between their respective records on a periodic basis to
arrive at the correct real property tax and special education tax
collectible balances; and
6.9.2. Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entry to fairly present the
actual tax collectible on the Statement of Financial Position based
on the certified list of tax delinquencies prepared by the Municipal
Treasurer.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to record semi-
expendable properties in the proper accounts to avoid the presentation of
misleading information in the Financial Statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare the
adjusting entries to derecognize the erroneous amount in the books of
the accounts of the LGU to reflect the correct balances of the affected
accounts.

We recommended that the:
9.9.1. Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entries to correct the
recognition of prior period income in the current year, and request
information/data/report from the MT for year-end undeposited
collections which will only be forwarded the following year; and
9.9.2. Municipal Treasurer report all collections for the year even including
those due for deposit in the ensuing year.

We recommended that Management require the GSO Designate to update his records and prepare property transfer report and for the Municipal Accountant to derecognize the properties issued to public schools upon transfer.
observations.managementAction:
The GSO updated his records and prepared property transfer report.

The Municipal Accountant has derecognized the properties to be issued/transferred to the public schools

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management conduct regular review of fund and property accountabilities of AOs, including those officers who are authorized signatories and counter signatories in the issuance of checks and approval of DVs, to determine the appropriate amount of their fidelity bonds.

We further recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to apply for the increase in the fidelity bond of the AOs to commensurate their accountabilities to provide adequate protection on the Municipality’s resources.
observations.managementAction:
The management regularly reviewed the fund and property accountabilities of accountable officers who are authorized signatories of checks and approved the DV to determine the appropriate amount of their fidelity bond.

When the fidelity bonds of AOs were renewed in July 2024, the respective bond were evaluated and appropriately increased the accountability.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management require the contractors to have the materials be tested by duly accredited DOST/BRS laboratories and submit the result of test(s) analysis to ensure the quality of materials used.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Engineer required the contractors to have the materials be tested by duly accredited DOST/ BRS laboratories and submit the result of test(s) analysis to ensure the quality of materials used.

For contracts awarded after the receipt of the audit observation, material testing was required from the Contractors on the materials used in the implementation of infrastructure projects

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all DVs are properly supported with complete documentation and in no case shall be paid without the necessary supporting documents.

Also, immediately submit to COA the lacking supporting documents for proper evaluation.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant ensured that all DVs are properly supported with complete documentation before payment.


The Municipal Engineer submitted the lacking documents.
observations.reason:
Payments were processed despite the non-submission of as-built plan and SWA from the contractor.

The lacking documents were submitted to the audit team in July 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Budget Officer (MBO) in coordination with the Municipal Engineer to review the unobligated appropriations of PPAs carried over as of CY 2024 Continuing Appropriations to determine which are eligible of continuing PPAs and thereafter, revert the unobligated appropriations of completed PPAs to be used for other potential development projects.
observations.managementAction: The MBO coordinated with the Municipal Engineer to review the unobligated appropriations of PPAs carried over as of CY 2023 Continuing Appropriations and reverted the same to the unappropriated surplus.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer deposit his collections regularly and intact in a cost-efficient way to prevent misappropriation of funds and possible loss.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer has since  deposited his collection regularly and intact to prevent misappropriation of funds or possible loss.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management investigate whether there were any misrepresentations made by the contractor with regard to its declaration on the personnel requirement for the government project. We further recommended management to instruct the BAC and its TWG to conduct thorough verification on the documents provided by the bidders to enable the agency to award projects to qualified bidders and guaranty the efficient implementation of the projects.
observations.managementAction: Management committed through its BAC to thoroughly verify the submitted documents of the bidders.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management instruct the MTO to collect the fees embodied in the Municipal Revenue Code and to establish strong coordination with the Agriculture Office for immediate identification whom to collect.
observations.managementAction: Management has since collected dog vaccination fee.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended
that the Municipal Mayor
require the Municipal
Accountant to prepare an
accounting entry by
debiting Due from Officers
and Employees and
crediting Prior Period
Adjustment in the amount
the of P14,400.00.
Moreover, require the
Municipal Treasurer to refund the whole amount
for being inattentive of his
duties and responsibilities.
Also, require the exercise
of due diligence in the
issuance of check by
religiously monitoring the
cash in bank balances
maintained by the
municipality in order to
avoid the incurrence of
bank charges and loss of
government funds.
observations.managementAction:
Recommendation was complied with by management.
An accounting entry was prepared by the Municipal Accountant under the JEV No. 2024-05-0470.
It was refunded under Official Receipt No. 4916219 dated December 27, 2024.
observations.reason:
Recommendation was complied with by management. It was refunded under Official Receipt No. 4916219 dated December 27, 2024.
Also, the Municipal Accountant will prepare an accounting entry and the Municipal Treasurer will do his best to settle the said amount. Henceforth, see to it that idle funds will be kept only in the HYSA.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended
that the Municipal Mayor
require the BAC to see to it
that all eligibility
requirements are present
and valid at the time of
submission and opening of
bids using a
nondiscretionary
“pass/fail” criterion in order
to ascertain that all
government contracts will
be awarded only to eligible
contractors or suppliers.
observations.managementAction:
Recommendation was complied with by management.
It was noted during post-audit that all eligibility requirements were present and valid at the time of submission and opening of bids as these were attached to the disbursement vouchers.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the BAC to submit justifications/explanations on the aforementioned lapses.
Moreover, require the BAC to issue bidding documents only after payment of the required fee unless uploaded in the PhilGEPS or agency websites in which case the bid proposals may be accepted outright after the payment of the required fee, thus impartiality in the procurement process is observed.
observations.managementAction:
Records showed that justification/explanation was submitted to the Audit Team.
Post-audit transactions disclosed that bidders were still able to prepare bid proposals prior to the actual acquisition of the bidding documents, hence the said documents were issued before the payment of the required fee.
observations.reason:
Justification/explanation was submitted to the Audit Team. However, post-audit disclosed that bidders were still able to prepare bid proposals prior to the actual acquisition of the bidding documents, hence the said documents were issued before the payment of the required fee.
Reiterated in Observation Number 14 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended
and the Local Chief
Executive agreed to require
the Municipal Accountant to compute and withhold
taxes from the payment of
BAC honoraria as mandated
in BIR Revenue
Regulations No. 11-2018
dated January 31, 2018 and
remit the same to the BIR
within the prescribed
period in order not to
deprive the National
Government of the
additional income that
could be utilized for
various programs, projects
and activities.
observations.managementAction: Post-audit of transactions disclosed that taxes for the payment of BAC honoraria were withheld and remitted.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation was complied with by management.
A copy of the revised confirmation letter issued by the BTr was submitted to the Office of the Auditor. The BTr made an error in the effectivity date which should be 2023-2024 instead of 2022-2023. Henceforth, renewal is made annually and before the expiration of the present bond.
The accountable officers were able to renew their fidelity bonds with effectivity date of May 31, 2024 to May 30, 2025.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Treasurer to approve employees’ loan applications to GFIs only within their capacity to pay and to disapprove applications for private lending institutions in order to meet the minimum net take home pay as required under Section 56 of General Appropriations Act of 2023. Thus, the employees could possibly meet the basic family needs before the next payroll period which could motivate them to work effectively and efficiently.
Also, the government will not act as collecting agent of the private companies, hence the additional expense of the government will be avoided.
observations.managementAction: Recommendation was complied with by management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management agreed to ensure that all vital information are provided in the project billboard, inform the AT in writing on the posting at the project site within ten days after the award of the contract and update the project status portion of the billboard based on the table prescribed in COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 20, 2023 in order to promote transparency in the implementation of infrastructure project, thus the people are well informed on matters of public concern.
observations.managementAction: Records showed that photos of the billboard installed in the project site were submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF not later than the 15th day after the end of each month and submit the same to the LDRRMO for the latter to reconcile with his records after which submit the same to the Auditor who will conduct review, verification and monitoring of the sources and utilization of LDRRMF, thus deficiencies if any, will be informed to Management for the latter’s immediate and appropriate corrective action thereof.
observations.managementAction: Records showed that monthly reports were submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor intensify the implementation of the PPAs funded out from the 20% DF and require the MPDC to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to monitor closely the implementation thereof so as to deliver to the constituents’ timely benefits that could be derived from these projects and to achieve a broader scope of development.
We also recommended management to reappropriate/realign projects which are not implementable due to some constraints including the amounts saved from projects which were already implemented.
In addition, we also recommended management to program only projects which are attainable and implementable within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction:
Review of the utilization report disclosed that 9 out of the 10 programs/projects under the current year appropriations were implemented in CY 2024.
Utilization report was not complied with. Some programs/projects were not yet reappropriated.
observations.reason: Review disclosed that 9 out of the 10 programs/projects under the current year appropriations were implemented in CY 2024. Utilization report was not complied with; amounts saved from completed projects/programs were not yet reappropriated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant, to obtain authorization from the SB in the opening of a separate bank account for the 20% DF. Thereafter, transfer the balance of the 20% DF from the bank account where it was deposited.
observations.managementAction:
SB Resolution 104-2022
