observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide for depreciation on recognized Road Networks accounts following the straight-line method in conformity with COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015 in line with Section 59 of PPSAS 17.

We reiterated to recommend that Management require the Property Officer – Designate to conduct a complete physical count of all the movable assets belonging to the municipality and prepare properly the Report of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment by classifying the properties counted according to its type. Also, we recommended that after the inventory taking, the Property Officer – Designate reconcile the result of the physical count with the accounting records and exert more effort in determining all discrepancies between physical and book inventories, if any. These shall be investigated, cleared and reconciled immediately.

We recommended that Local Finance Committee and Municipal School Board stop the utilization of SEF to programs, projects and activities not included in the provisions under RA 7160 and the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1-2017 dated January 19, 2017 in order to maximize the benefits that the students and teachers could avail therefrom.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) open the bids received immediately after the deadline for the submission of bids as scheduled, unless postponed due to justifiable reasons. Moreover, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the BAC to strictly observe the provisions of the Revised 2016 IRR of RA 9184 and to discharge faithfully their respective duties and functions as BAC Members with utmost responsibility and integrity.
We recommended that the Municipality immediately prepare the implementing guidelines in order to facilitate the proper implementation and collection of fees embodied in the Municipal Tax Code.
We recommended that Management stop the practice of allowing employees’ claim for registration and similar fees from the government on meetings not covered by NBC 563 dated April 26, 2016.
We recommended that Management stop from paying registration fees directly to the hotel venue, but rather require the official receipts from the sponsoring agency for complete documentation.

We recommended that Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) carefully scrutinize and review the applications for leave of the municipal officials and employees in accordance with the provisions of the Omnibus Rules on Leave before endorsing it to the Agency Head for approval.

We recommended that Management instruct the payroll in-charge to strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9679 otherwise known as the Home Development Mutual Fund Act of 2009 in observing the maximum limit of Pag-IBIG contributions employer’s share of P100.00 per month per official/employee, unless amended. Also, require the officials/employees to sign a waiver signifying their intention whenever they opt to increase their personal share for Pag-IBIG contributions to be remitted to HDMF.

We recommended that management recognize in the book of accounts and in the RPCPPE the cellular phone units availed under a postpaid plan subscription and issue a corresponding PAR to the accountable officer concerned.

We recommended that Management instruct the in-charge in the preparation of Purchase Orders (POs) to strictly follow the requirements set forth under COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996 for proper and timely verification of transactions to avoid any confusion on the part of the supplier during deliveries, and during inspection and acceptance on the part of the agency.

We recommended that Management carefully evaluate the viability and sustainability of the Livelihood Development Program by taking into consideration its profitability, productivity and effectiveness to avoid wastage of government funds and discontinue the implementation of the program, if found unsustainable to avoid possible disallowance.

We recommended that management ensure the effective, efficient and timely implementation of the proposed projects under the 20% Development Fund through close monitoring and supervision to fully maximize the benefits to the intended recipients.

We reiterated to recommend that management revert back the amount of P1,351,784.65 to the general fund’s un-appropriated surplus and make it available for other social services thru subsequent enactment by the local sanggunian in compliance to COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012. Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw the necessary adjusting entry to properly reflect the correct balance of the Trust Liabilities account as at year-end.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort to locate records of these receivables. If such efforts exerted are futile, Management may file a request for authority to write-off said accounts except the accounts Receivables – Disallowances/Charges and Other Receivables arising from cash shortage, to the COA Audit Team Leader and/or Supervising Auditor.  The request shall be supported with complete documents enumerated in Section 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Enforce the payment of fuel, oil and lubricants directly to the supplier in observance of sound principles of  internal control on the disbursement process and in order to withhold the taxes due to the government;
Assign a knowledgeable personnel to check the details or review the propriety and completeness of information on the trip tickets prior to official trips and  to regularly submit Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption to the Auditor thru the Municipal Accountant for purposes of verification on the reasonableness of fuel consumption; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to review and verify the completeness of documentary requirements for claims against municipal funds before affixing her signature on Box A of each disbursement voucher to ascertain the validity and propriety of disbursements made.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Bids and Awards Committee to comply with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001 regarding the submission of copies of perfected contracts and purchase orders with their supporting documents to the Auditor within five (5) working days from execution and issuance thereof for review and evaluation so that management will be immediately informed of defects and deficiencies noted, if any and to facilitate the inspection of completed projects and deliveries of goods.

We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant, cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents and justifications on the deficiencies observed for further evaluation of the transactions and avoid suspension in audit. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that disbursement vouchers are duly supported with the required basic documents to ensure their validity and propriety.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents to facilitate the technical evaluation of the projects and avoid supension in audit. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant require all the necessary documents pertaining to the implementation of infrastructrure projects before payment shall be made to establish the validity and propriety of transactions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, through the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer, include the utilization of unexpended balance for DRRM, which is under the Trust Liability – DRRM, in the LDRRMFIP for programs/projects/activities related to disaster management and prevention. Moreover, strictly abide with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and refrain from charging expenses not related to disaster risk reduction and management activities to LDRRM Fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause the prompt implementation of all projects/programs funded out of the tobacco excise tax in order to provide the constituents, especially the tobacco farmers the early benefits that could be derived therefrom.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and other implementing offices to review the composition of the 20% Development Fund (DF); identify the savings from fully implemented projects and those which are no longer prioritized; and subject to the provisions of the Local Government Code and the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1, reappropriate to current/existing priority development projects. Likewise, instruct the MPDC, Municipal Engineer and other concerned offices to coordinate and strategize to implement fully all the programs/projects under the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that Management ensure the optimal utilization of the 20% Development Fund to attain desirable socio-economic and environmental outcomes of the Municipality and intended beneficiaries. It should not be used for any purpose beyond those expressly prescribed under the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01 dated February 22, 2017 in order to avoid possible applicable sanctions.

We have recommended and Management assured that the Municipal Accountant to:
Reclassify the completed projects from CIP accounts to its respective class of PPE for proper disclosure of the same in the financial statements;
Make necessary adjustments relative to the current and prior years unrecorded depreciation expense of any finished CIP-agency asset; and
Ensure that all CIP projects are reconciled with the Report on PPAs prepared by the Municipal Engineer.

We have recommended and Management agreed to:
Immediately cause the disposal of the unserviceable properties in accordance with Section 79 of PD 1445; and
Schedule a second public auction for the sale of the said properties.  In case of failure, the property may be sold at a private or negotiated sale in accordance with the Manual on the Disposal of Government Property (National Budget Circular 425 dated January 28, 1992).

We have recommended and the Management agreed to adhere strictly with the above-cited provisions of IPSAS 1, as well as the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-009 on the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs. Thus, it should record the salaries of its personnel under contract of service using the appropriate Professional and General Services accounts.

We have recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to adhere strictly with the above-cited provisions of the International Public Sector Accounting Standard (IPSAS) 2, as well as the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-009 on the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs. Likewise, we recommend that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the insurance expense account and recognize the Prepaid Insurance account for the unexpired portion thereof.

We have recommended the Head of the Procuring Entity to direct the BAC to:
Submit justification in declaring the contractors as Lowest Calculated Responsive Bidder despite the foregoing deficiencies noted; and
Judiciously conduct post-qualification by verifying, validating, and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid and ensure that the Post-Qualification Report contains in-depth results on the verification of technical requirements.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to make a representation with the Provincial Treasurer’s Office to seek assistance in the enforcement of collection of delinquent taxes, especially those long over-due.  Moreover, to ask assistance from the Barangay Officials for them to inform the heirs of the deceased delinquent taxpayers or occupants of the properties to pay their delinquent taxes.

We have recommended Management to:
Prioritize the installation of the water meters to facilitate the measurement of the volume of water delivered to a household or property;
Monitor and evaluate both the physical and financial aspects of the operation of the SALINTUBIG Projects including its functionality, effectiveness and efficiency for the benefit of the intended end-users; and
Prepare a reasonable work plan based from the result of the M&E activities to attain sustainability of the SALINTUBIG Projects and its eventual conversion as a local economic enterprise.

We have recommended Management to:
Strictly comply with the above-cited provisions of RA 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations; and
Keep themselves abreast with the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular 2017-1 to ensure that their projects under the 20% DF partakes the allowed nature in actual and in name.

We have recommended that Management strictly adhere to the guidelines of NDRRMC, DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 in the allocation and use of the LDRRM fund and ensure that the fund is allocated and utilized for priority projects and activities that support the goals under the four (4) thematic areas of the DRRM.

We have recommended the Local Chief Executive and Solid Waste Management Board to:
Prioritize the establishment of sanitary landfill by constant and consistent follow up with the concerned agencies for it to finally materialize and so that they can already gear up for the next step for its construction;
Strictly enforce waste segregation at source in order to achieve the primary objective to protect the environment and public health from substantial, present, and potential hazard of solid wastes; and
Promote Solid Waste Management Awareness through proactive Information and Education Campaigns (IEC) in coordination with the Barangay Solid Waste Management Committees and academic institutions.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Local GAD Focal Person through the Municipal Planning and Development Office to comply with the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2013-01 and 2016-01 on the establishment of a gender responsive monitoring and evaluation system through the GAD M&E Team’s performance of its functions to evaluate the outcomes of the LGU GAD policies, programs, and projects.

We have recommended that the utilization of SEF be in accordance with the rules and regulations as enunciated under DepEd, DBM, DILG Joint Circular and RA 7160. Further, we recommend to the Local School Board to prioritize programs, projects and activities as stipulated in the abovementioned provisions.

We have recommended the Management to:
Dutifully and promptly remit all barangay shares on RPT on a quarterly basis within five (5) days after the end of each quarter as provided under Section 271 (d) of the Local Government Code; and
Maintain separate Subsidiary Ledgers for the RPT shares of each of the barangay.

We have recommended Management to:
Prepare and process the payrolls of regular personnel on a monthly basis. These payrolls should be prepared in two (2) sets, reflecting both the 1st and 2nd quincena of the month. The net pay should be arrived at by adding the basic salary and all allowances less total deductions divided by two (2); and
Prepare payslips and distribute the same, by office, to every employee to serve as personal record of earnings and deductions for the period they have rendered service.

We recommended that Management exhaust all possible means to document the legality of ownership over the parcels of land through Transfer Certificates of Title (TCTs) in the name of the Municipality to avoid third party claims/land dispute and to ensure that the LGU has legal rights over all recognized assets in the financial statements.

We recommended to Management the following courses of actions:
Require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer/Supply Officer to reconcile their records and determine the value of the 16 units of unserviceable properties which may facilitate the determination of the current appraisal value of the said properties; and
Require the head of the Inventory Disposal Committee to facilitate the disposal of the remaining unserviceable properties of the agency in accordance with the requirements and procedures provided in Sections 76 and 79 of PD 1445 to generate additional funds for the agency’s operations, prevent said unserviceable properties from further deterioration and loss of value, and allow the agency to use the space occupied by the unserviceable properties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive provide a stockroom for the use of the Municipal Treasurer to have a proper and centralized system of inventory purchases and issuances.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant strictly observe the requirements under Chapter 7, Volume I of the NGAS Manual for LGUs by properly accomplishing and maintaining stock cards, stock ledger cards, Requisition Issue Slips, Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued and Inventory Custodian Slips for the proper recording of related expenses and inventory balances using the moving average method of costing.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to exert effort to collect the receivables from debtors/agencies. Those that can no longer be accounted or settled may be requested for write-off subject to the requirements and procedures of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, and to reconcile and clear the books of accounts of dormant receivables and fund transfers.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the balances of fund transfers in order to effect the liquidation of the fund transfers from National Government Agencies and the immediate return of the unexpended balances of the same to the source agencies concerned pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal Accountant to observe strictly the generally accepted accounting principles in the recording of the amortization on LGU’s loans and the accrual of interest expense relative thereto for fair presentation of affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management require the immediate submission of the lacking documents to the audit team for verification and audit purposes in accordance with Section 4 (6) of PD 1445 and Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to preclude the issuance of Notice of Suspension.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman require the BAC Secretariat to post the Notices of Award and Notices to Proceed together with the approved contracts for the procurement of infrastructure projects, goods and services in the PhilGEPS and agency website, if any, within the prescribed period as required under  Sections 37.1.6, 37.4.2 and 54.3 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act (RA) No. 9184 for transparency in government procurement.

We recommended that Management exert more effort to ensure the submission of all the perfected contracts together with the complete supporting documents within the period prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 to enable the auditor to conduct timely review and evaluation of the transactions.

We recommended Management the following courses of actions:
Evaluate strictly the need for consultants taking into consideration their expertise, training or education whose skills are not possessed by the regular employees of the LGU; and
Submit the duly approved Accomplishment Report providing the details of the services rendered by the Consultant and documents to support the various deficiencies noted in the hiring of Consultant for evaluation of the audit team, and to avoid disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the officials concerned to strictly observe the policy on full disclosure as provided in DILG MC No. 2013-140, as amended by DILG MC 2019-149, COA Circular No. 2013-004 and other relevant laws, rules and regulations for transparency and to secure the right of the people to information on matters of public concern.

We recommended that management refrain from incurring advertising expenses for purposes other than the authorized items in R.A. 7160, R.A. 9184, COA Circular No. 2013-004, COA Circular No. 2012-003 and other relevant laws, rules and regulations to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the employees concerned to submit the necessary documents to support the payment of honoraria to lecturers, resource persons and facilitators pursuant to Section 5.7.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and DBM Circular No. 2007-1.
We also recommended that Management submit justification citing their basis in granting honoraria to agency officials/employees during the “Formulation of Executive Legislative / Capacity Development Agenda”, otherwise, refund the relative amount of P20,500.00 paid to them.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to require the suppliers to submit original and valid copies of Sales Invoice and/or Official Receipts on various procurement of goods/services in compliance with BIR Revenue Regulations No. 18-2012, BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 2-2014 and Section 39 (1) of the Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445. Likewise, ensure strict observance of the said regulations on future disbursements to be made.

We recommended that the Accounting Office, in cooperation with the Treasury Office, ensure the complete submission of accounts within the prescribed period for timely verification and audit of accounts pursuant to Section 7.2.1 of the 2009 of the Rules and Regulations on the Settlement of Accounts, and Section 39 (1) and (3) of P.D 1445.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to:
Require the Municipal Budget Officer to prepare and submit monthly Statement of Appropriations, Allotments, Obligations and Balances not later than the twentieth day after the end of each month;
Instruct the Budget Officer and Accountant to perform quarterly reconciliation of the budget and actual amounts in order to prepare periodically the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts; and
Ensure that the year-end set of financial statements are submitted on or before the 14th day of February of the ensuing year.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the HRMO to implement immediately the recording of daily attendance using the biometric system in order to hasten the internal control relative thereto and institute a comprehensive internal control system guidelines for its implementation including, but not limited to the procedures to be undertaken if an employee failed to enter his/her fingerprint in the system, and during system shutdowns. Likewise, the monthly record of attendance (DTR) to be submitted to the Audit Team shall be supported with the log schedules generated from the biometric machine.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Person to ensure that the annual GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report strictly follow the rules laid down under PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01 specifically on the presentation of attributed programs.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer increase the amount withheld from the salaries of the delinquent employees to settle the loans which have been outstanding for a long period of time.  Likewise, we recommended to continue sending demand letters and employ aggressive collection strategies to enforce collection of the loans especially to the borrowers related to the employees of the LGU.  In case of non-collection thereof, resort to legal action in the collection of overdue receivables to avoid possible loss of government funds.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the MSWDO to continue to follow up with the ECCD Council for the delivery of necessary materials and equipment for the operation of NCDC. For the meantime, discuss with the officials concerned the possibility of transferring temporarily the resources being used in the existing Poblacion Day Care Center to the NCDC in order for the benefit of the young learners.
We further recommended that the Management submit ECCD Council’s approval on the modification of the NCDC’s interior layout which provided for MSWDO’s office space; otherwise, the Local Chief Executive instruct the transfer of the said office to other building/area.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Treasurer to continue and improve the collection strategies on taxes due and collectible by sending RPTOP’s and notice of delinquency to taxpayers and to conduct an information dissemination regarding the importance of paying taxes promptly and the consequences of non-payment of taxes; and
If circumstances warrant, impose the provisions of Sections 258 and 260 of the Local Government Code of 1991 in the collections of delinquent taxes to satisfy taxes, penalties and costs with the following:
File civil suit with the proper court; and
Levy on real property and sale of delinquent property or a usable portion thereof at public auction.

We recommended that the Collecting Officer in-charge provide a cut-off of her collections during the last working day of the year and allot a time to deposit all collections at the Authorized Government Depository Banks of the Municipality as prescribed under Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 to avoid the exposure of the same to possible risk of loss.
Likewise, the Municipal Accountant ensure the monthly conduct of reconciliation of her Cash-Local Treasury Ledger balances with that of the Cashbook-Collections of the Municipal Treasurer and submit a Statement of Reconciliation to the Audit Team every given date.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended to the Management  the following:
The Municipal Assessor to furnish the Municipal Treasurer the assessment roll, together with a list of assessed real property indicating the names of the concerned taxpayers in whose name the property is declared and assessed during the period.  The said list shall include information such as but not limited to the following: (1) Name of RPT/SET taxpayer and complete address; (2) Descriptions of the assessed property;  (3) Tax Declaration Number;  (4) Market Value; (5) Assessed Value; (6) Type of Property, Estimated Useful Life (if applicable); and (7) Actual Use of Property;
The Municipal Treasurer to provide the Municipal Accountant a summary copy of her collections for the year showing the details: (1) Name of RPT/SET taxpayer and complete address; (2) Tax Declaration Number; (3) Total Amount Paid; (4) Detailed Breakdown/Explanation of Actual Amount Paid; and (5) a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year.  Likewise, prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant the list of taxpayers with delinquencies for the years 2004 to 2018 as of December 31, 2019 to support the year-end ledger balances;
The Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjustments of RPT/SET Receivables once the list of delinquent taxpayers is received from the Municipal Treasurer to reflect the correct balance of these receivable accounts. Likewise, maintain and update the corresponding subsidiary ledger for each taxpayer to facilitate monitoring and preparation of necessary reports/schedules.
The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the schedule of delinquent RPT/SET taxpayers so that the latter could notify delinquent taxpayers on time.
The Municipal Treasurer prepare a statement of the reason or reasons for such non-collection or non-payment and submit to the Sanggunian concerned together with the list of taxpayers with delinquencies with a request for assistance in the enforcement of the remedies for collection provided in RA No. 7160 in order for the Municipality to have funds for other projects that will benefit its constituents.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended to  the Management the following:
the Municipal Accountant to:
Ensure that the officers and employees with outstanding or unliquidated cash advances should not be granted additional cash advances, until the previous ones are settled, to preclude accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.
Ensure that all regulations pertaining to cash advances should be strictly enforced.
Monitor the outstanding cash advances of officials and employees and demand immediate settlement after its purpose has been served.
Issue demand letter to settle the unliquidated cash advances.
the Municipal Treasurer to:
If warranted, withhold the salaries of those who persistently fail or refuse to comply despite repeated demand for liquidation and/or file appropriate administrative and criminal charges against them as instituted in the sanctions provided for under paragraphs 9.2 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 to compel liquidation.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Management conduct an investigation on the status of these NGOs/POs and various individuals and discuss with the appropriate committee in the Sangguniang Bayan any remedial actions that will be undertaken to the advantage of the Municipality.  Moreover, please be guided with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-05 dated December 19, 2016 for the write-off of dormant receivables.
The Management commented the following:
“The Management made a request to the Office of the President thru SB Resolution No. 2019-__ for the condemnation of the loan extended to the Municipality which represents the fund released to these various NGOs/POs since the collectability of the said receivables was very low and as of this date there was no response or feedback on the status of this request. Once the Office of the President grants the said request of the LGU, the management will prepare the necessary documents for the possible write-off of the said account. On the other hand, demand letter was sent to the various beneficiaries but still no payment was made.
Rest assured that the management will make the necessary actions to implement the recommendations of the Commission.”
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer, after exerting efforts in collecting the receivables from NGAs such as sending demand letters, submit to the Commission on Audit a request for the write-off of receivables and ensure completeness of required documents to facilitate action thereof pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 9, 2016.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator explain the inclusion of the Materials Quality Control Cost in the contract otherwise the amount shall be disallowed in audit.  Likewise, we recommend that the Management ensure compliance with the specific standards in the preparation of program of works and detailed estimates of future infrastructure project proposals in order to avoid incurrence of unnecessary expenditures.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect the corresponding depreciation as soon as the corresponding useful life of road and road components are identified and make necessary disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements. Moreover, keep and maintain subsidiary records for road and road components for every road network as required in the Circular.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant undertake a thorough review and analysis of the details of the payable accounts and effect the necessary adjustments to revert to the appropriate fund the balance of liability accounts which could not be supported with valid claims.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that all financial accounts and reports shall be submitted to the Commission on Audit within the prescribed period of ten (10) days from the beginning of the ensuing month. Likewise, the Municipal Mayor consider the possibility of assigning additional personnel in the Treasury and Accounting to facilitate submission of accounts and also improve the Municipality’s operations especially in adherence to pertinent laws, rules and regulations.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Treasurer send demand letters to delinquent taxpayers and conduct an information campaign for stricter tax collection measures to all real property owners within the Municipality with emphasis of granting discounts when paid before due dates and remind taxpayers of remedies that can be exercised by the Municipality in case of delinquencies with the possibility of losing their property for not paying at all.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that Management (a) require the contractor to engage the catch-up program in order to fast track the completion of the project to avoid rescindment of contract as stipulated in Section 68 of RA 9184; (b) conduct an inventory of work accomplished by the contractor and impose liquidated damages at least equal to one-tenth of one percent (0.1%) of the cost of the unperformed portion for every day of delay; and (c) intensify monitoring of project in order to avoid recurrence of delay to other projects.
As part of the Municipality’s compliance, they provided the Audit Team a scanned copy of DV No.100-2019-10-2979 dated October 30, 2019 for the final payment of subject-project above, showing the P18,936.44 liquidated damages as deduction from the claim.

We recommended that (a) the Management ensure the optimal utilization of the 20% Development Fund by fast-tracking the project implementation of on-going projects, reviewing those that may no longer be utmost priority  and reprogramming the same to other more important PPAs economically beneficial to the constituents; and (b) the Municipal Development Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, and the Municipal Engineer closely monitor and strongly pursue the timely and full implementation of all PPAs and regularly submit status reports to the Municipal Mayor in order that appropriate measures can be instituted.
The Management presented and commented the following:
It is recognized that the Local Development Fund is a required allocation of our LGU from the annual Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA) to finance the LGU’s priority developmental projects, thus supporting the Philippine Developmental Plan and Investment Programs. Withal, rest assured that the fund is being used prudently and utilized optimally according to the guidelines set by authorities, and making certain that necessary services will reach the people; thus promoting the general welfare of our constituents.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Management fast track the prompt implementation of all projects/programs funded out of the tobacco excise tax in order to give the constituents early benefits that could be derived therefrom.
The Management commented the following:
“ I.	 The 2019 Tobacco Excise Tax – Share of the Municipality of Burgos from the CY 2016 Collection of Burley and Native Tobacco Excise Tax under RA No. 8240, as Amended, amounting to nineteen million eight hundred thirty-four thousand seven hundred eighty-seven pesos (P19,834,787.00). Out of the appropriated amount, only one million nine hundred ninety-two thousand six hundred pesos (P1,992,600.00) was obligated for the Financial Assistance to Tobacco Farmer Leaders as listed by the National Tobacco Administration for CY 2019.
In view of the foregoing circumstances, the Local Government Unit of Burgos was only able to obligate 10.04% of the total fund. We understand that we have responsibility over the affairs, transactions and operations of the local government unit. However, the total amount was only received by the municipality on December 16, 2019 with Authority to Debit No. 2019-12-029; which is almost the close of the year and only less than fifteen days to the date of your performance audit.
II. The continuing appropriations for the tobacco excise tax fund amounting to five hundred thirty-six thousand sixty-eight pesos and forty-three centavos (P536,068.43) is pending approval to the DBM on request for realignment to infrastructure project (concreting of farm to market road. The fund was initially programmed for the rehabilitation of NFA Building to be used as an office for the Tobacco Farmers Association. However, said association is now inactive, thus the LGU’s request for realignment in order to fully optimize the utilization of such resources.
It is acknowledged that we have the duty to ensure prompt implementation of projects for basic service delivery in relation with the approved development plan of the municipality. Furthermore, we ensure that the listed programs and projects will be strictly utilized in accordance with RA No. 7171 and RA No. 8240, as amended by RA No. 10351, and other applicable accounting and auditing rules.
However, our top priority today is to wage the war against our common enemy: the Corona Virus Disease (CoVid-19). The challenges we currently face in the local government are grave. There is a clear and present danger we must prevent, address, and contain with vet limited time and vast logistical demand. Please understand the difficulties posed by the current circumstances.”
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to:
Monitor the utilization of LDRRMF focusing on programs, projects and activities (PPAs) requiring the highest priority in terms of urgency and/or impact on four (4) thematic areas of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management;
Ensure efficient and effective implementation of all programmed projects identified during the budget year to optimize the utilization of the fund and to warrant that goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities be achieved; and
Direct the MDRRM Officer to submit monthly the Monthly Report on Utilization of LDRRM Fund to COA and DILG.
The Management commented the following:
“PPAs of LDRRM Plan for CY2019 were implemented and utilized at a rate of 58.53% versus total appropriation of P4,444,557.40. LGU has allocated P3,111,190.18 as 70% Mitigation Fund and of which P2,601,264.53 was utilized which corresponds to 83.61%. The 30% QRF was not utilized due to non-declaration or non-occurrence of calamity for CY 2019.
Rest assured that the management will make the necessary actions to implement the recommendations of the Commission.”
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant support the transaction noted in writing and adopt internal control measures to preclude the incurrence of unnecessary expenditures in the form of bank service charges.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD focal person and technical working group to ensure full implementation of the plans, programs and activities which are gender responsive and compliant with JMC Nos. 2013-01 and 2016-01 to continuously provide socio-economic services to the fund’s intended beneficiaries.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Management intensify its efforts to have the 10-Year Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission to achieve its vision of having an economically stable, healthy, safe, adaptive, resilient God loving and law-abiding citizenry living in a peaceful, orderly and ecologically sound environment with a responsive and dynamic leadership.
The Management commented that their LGU has successfully defended the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan covering the years 2019-2027 during the deliberation conducted on September 5, 2019. On September 6, 2019, a resolution approving the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan for the Municipality covering the years 2018-2027 under NSWMC Resolution No. 1274 series of 2019 was received only by the LGU last February 21, 2020 through the Office of the Environmental Management Bureau (EMB) Regional Office 02. Copy of the resolution was submitted to the Audit Team for evaluation.
The Audit Team commended the LGU for their efforts to accomplish the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan and the success to defend the said plan. The Audit team shall continually monitor the compliance of the LGU in the content of the resolution regarding the submission of final version of the plan, with a Sangguniang Bayan Resolution and an annual progress report on the strategies implemented, as well as the accomplishments, to the NSWMC, through its Secretariat.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) continue being compliant with the revenue regulations on the withholding of taxes from compensation of employees and creditable taxes for payments to suppliers and contractors and the remittance thereof within the prescribed period; (b) support an explanation through appropriate disclosure in the notes to financial statements and detailed computation showing if the balance reported pertains to unremitted taxes withheld for the month of December 2019 otherwise resolve and reconcile any carry-over discrepancy on the Due to BIR account balance and effect immediately the remittance of the overdue balance.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Management continue to (a) withhold and remit the mandatory PHIC contributions and (b) direct the Municipal Accountant to support the year-end balance a disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements if it pertains to December 2019 withheld amounts or from prior years balance and conduct review or reconciliation for the noted balance in order that services to be availed of by the employees from Philhealth will not be delayed or affected, if any.
The Audit Team shall monitor the compliance of the LGU with the audit recommendations.

1.9     We recommended and  Management  agreed  to  establish  a  strong
        internal control in the checking of the Collectors’ remittances for
        early detection of delayed and incomplete remittance. The Municipal
        Treasury  Office  should  also  enforce  daily  turn-over  by   the
        Collector to the Liquidating Officer to avoid possible loss, misuse
        and misappropriation of government funds.

2.7     We recommended and Management agreed to open an account with the
        nearest bank within Siargao Island;  establish  a  strong  internal
        control in the implementation of weekly deposit of  collections  to
        the  depository  banks  to  avoid   possible   loss,   misuse   and
        misappropriation  of  government  funds.  And   ensure   that   the
        collections deposited  is  intact  and  complete;  and  maintain  a
        cashbook for the Trust Fund – Cash in Treasury.

3.7     We recommended and Management agreed to instruct  the  Municipal
        Treasurer to establish a strong internal control in the  remittance
        of the collections, and the Municipal Accountant to  recognize  the
        income on the exact year when the revenue  is  collected  to  avoid
        misleading information in the Financial Statements.

4.7     We recommended and Accounting Office agreed to:


     1.  Properly maintain the Subsidiary Ledgers  (SL)  to  those  accounts
        that are  composed of several specific  accounts/category/accounting
        responsibility;


        2.  Ensure  that  beginning  balances  are  carried  forward  in   a
           particular year in the SL should be  the  same  with  the  ending
           balances in the previous year;


        3. Compute the running balance after every transaction;  discontinue
           the use of pencils in recording transactions; and if  attainable,
           employ the use of  e-NGAS  to  assure  correctness,  reliability,
           completeness, and timeliness in  recording  government  financial
           transactions and to generate financial reports in accordance with
           the policies and procedures of the NGAS.

5.8     We recommended and Municipal Accountant agreed to  prepares  the
        necessary adjustments to reconcile the book balance with  the  bank
        balance particularly those  reconciling  items  whose  nature  were
        already identified and ensure that proper procedures be  undertaken
        before changes be made in the records.

6.4     We recommended and Management agreed to strictly adhere with the
        requirements and provisions of existing laws concerning  Accounting
        for  Supplies  and  Materials  to strengthen the internal  control
        for  supplies  and  materials  and:


        a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to stop the practice  of  using
           expense method in recording  supplies  and  materials  purchases,
           maintain an inventory account using perpetual  inventory  system,
           prepare supplies ledger card per stock number, make  an  internal
           control policy on the maintenance of stock cards on the MDRRMO,
           MTO and MHO to establish authority on the control of inventory of
           the same and recognize and measure  reliably  the  unconsumed  or
           unused supplies and materials as part of  the  inventory  account
           and adjust to its corresponding expense account;


        b. Inform the Municipal Procurement Officer to maintain supplies and
           materials stock cards per stock number  and  prepare  Summary  of
           Supplies and Materials Issued weekly and submit the same  to  the
           Municipal Accountant for recording purposes to fairly present its
           inventory accounts in the financial statements; and


        c. Notify the Inventory Committee  to  conduct  the  required  semi-
           annual physical count, reconcile the same with  the  stock  cards
           and supplies ledger cards and submit  the  RPCI  to  the  auditor
           concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year  for
           the first and second semester, respectively.

          Below is the Audit team’s proposed adjusting journal entry:
          a. To adjust the supplies and materials expense account:
                    Dr. Inventory Account           xx
                          Cr. Expense Account                xx

7.14    We recommended and Management agreed to:


      a. instruct the Municipal Procurement Officer  to  maintain  property
           cards in addition to  her  tasks  expressly  recommended  in  the
           previous AOM for Supplies and Materials;


      b.   instruct the Municipal Accountant to train one of her staff  who
           will be tasked to maintain the required PPE  and  WOABS  Property
           Ledger Card and subsidiary ledgers  of  the  PPE  and  Biological
           Asset accounts; and


        d. reconcile the RPCPPE with the property  cards  of  the  Municipal
           Procurement  Officer,  PPE  ledger  cards  with   the   Municipal
           Accountant and any adjustments must take effect  between  records
           also with the subsidiary ledgers for each PPE accounts.

8.6     We recommended and Municipal Accountant agreed to:


     1. Adhere strictly with laws and provisions concerning proper usage of
           accounts for every transactions and events;


        2. Transfer the amount from  Due  from Officers  and  Employees  to
           Advances to Special Disbursing Officer; and

        3. Use the Advances to Special Disbursing Officer  account  for  the
           recording of cash  advances  for  confidential  and  intelligence
           funds from the Municipality.

9.8     We recommended and Municipal Accountant  agreed  to  adjust  the
        loans payable’s  audited  carrying  amount  of  ₱20,358,674.27  and
        present the current portion of its Loans Payable in the face of the
        LGU’s financial  statements  to  provide  users  of  the  financial
        statements’ fair presentation  as  to  liquidity  and  solvency  is
        concerned.

10.9    We recommended and Municipal Accountant agreed to:


        a. review all journal  entries  to  ensure  that  transactions  are
           properly recorded to reflect in  the  financial  statements  the
           actual results of operation for every calendar year; and


        b. ensure that only actual and valid expenses shall be taken up  in
           the  books  using  the  appropriate  expenses  accounts  and  be
           recorded in the period to which these expenses relate.

12. We recommended and Municipal Accountant – Designate agreed to  make
        the necessary adjusting entries  presented  above  to  correct  the
        erroneous accounting entries in the  GF  books  and  establish  the
        Trust Liability-DRRM in the  TF  books.   Further,  the  Accounting
        Office shall follow strictly the recording and reporting guidelines
        for LDRRMF as prescribed in COA Circular 2012-002  dated  September
        12, 2012.

12.11 We recommended and Management agreed to:


        a. require the Solid Waste Management Board to perform its functions
           in accordance with RA 9003;


        b. strictly implement  the  Segregation  at  Source  and  Segregated
           Collection to all barangays;


        c. provide sufficient funding not only from local sources of the LGU
           but also secure grants from other government agencies; and


        d.  enforce  the  collection  system  on  fines  and  penalties   of
           violators.

13.10   We recommended and Management agreed to strictly adhere  to  the
        rules and regulations of RA 9184, instruct all Implementing Offices
        to refrain from performing procurement activities and  require  the
        BAC to undertake all procurement  of  goods  and  services  of  the
        Municipality.

14.11 We recommended and Management agreed to:


        a. Require officers and employees to submit the  payrolls  with  the
           approved DTRs and its mandated format  by  CSC,  application  for
           leave and other pertinent supporting  documents  as  required  in
           Sections 1.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and;


        b. Direct concerned personnel who prepares the  payroll  to  exclude
           those employees, who failed to submit their DTRs, in the  payroll
           to adopt the “NO DTR, NO SALARY” policy and to ensure the  prompt
           submission of their DTRs.

7. We recommended and Management agreed to  require  the  payroll  in-
        charged to stop the granting of fix rate of ₱150.00  and  ₱1,500.00
        for Laundry and Subsistence  Allowance,  respectively,  and  follow
        strictly the rules and regulations on the  grant  of  compensation-
        related Magna Carta benefits to PHWs and MSWDO based on their Daily
        Time Records and/or actual number of workdays rendered.

16.8    We recommended and Management agreed to:


     a. Submits immediately the 99 DVs and its related supporting documents
        and      the deficient necessary supporting  documents  of  the  20
        DVs; and


        b. Requires the Municipal Accountant not to process  the  DVs  with
           incomplete documentation and to maintain Logbook  for  all  out-
           going and incoming documents for control purposes to ensure that
           all documents of the agency will be properly safeguarded against
           possible loss and unauthorized access.

17.10   We recommended  and  Management  agreed  to  require  the  LDRRM
        Officer to install mechanism that will monitor  the  implementation
        of the PPAs  identified  in  the  LDRRM  Plan  and  fast-track  its
        implementation  and  formulate  or  propose   programs   that   are
        achievable to ensure implementation thereof within the budget year.

18.11   We recommended and Management agreed to  stop  the  practice  of
        allowing job order personnel to  travel  outside  the  Municipality
        with travel allowances and/or per diems. Authority to travel should
        be  granted  only  when  these  are  urgent,  extremely  necessary,
        involves the minimum expenditure and are beneficial to  the  agency
        concerned and/or the country without resulting to depletion of  LGU
        resources, increased MOOE and misuse of government funds.

19.7    We recommended and Management agreed to require  the  GAD  Focal
        Point System to attend trainings/workshop on how to apply the  HGDG
        tool or consult an expert that could perform gender analysis  using
        the said tool.

20.8    We recommended and Management agreed to instruct  the  Municipal
        Budget and Accounting Offices to refrain  from  utilizing  the  GAD
        Fund for expenses not within the approved GAD Plan and Budget.

21.8    We recommended and Management agreed to  direct  the  GAD  Focal
        Point  System  to  properly  plan   and   regularly   monitor   the
        implementation of GAD PPAs and maximize the utilization of the  GAD
        budget to achieve the objective of  the  government  regarding  the
        institutionalization of GAD in the community.

We recommended that Management adhere existing rules and regulations in the granting of cash advances in order to protect the government funds from possible loss, misuse and/or misappropriations. Further, we recommended that Management refrain from granting cash advances to employees in behalf of their co-employees in order to establish and monitor properly the accountability of each and every employee.
observations.managementAction: No similar transaction was noted during the period audited.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management stop the practice of granting special purpose cash advances to elected municipal officials. Instead, grant such cash advances only to properly designated employees covered with sufficient bond to prevent possible exposure of government funds to misuse and/or misappropriations.
observations.managementAction: Special purpose cash advances were granted to the Municipal Treasurer, being the designated special disbursing officer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the management the following: (1) The SB Secretary to furnish the Human Resource Management Office (HRMO) Certificate of Service for the period covered of the payroll of the elected officials under the legislative department for consolidation; (2) The HRMO to: a) check the correctness of the entries and signatures in the DTR; b) ensure the completeness of all employees’ DTRs and CS Form 6, if any; and c) consolidate the same, together with the Certificate of Service/Attendance submitted by the SB Secretary, before forwarding to the Accounting Office for processing; and (3) The Municipal Accountant to process only payrolls with complete supporting documents in order to prove that services had been rendered by the officials/employees.
observations.managementAction: Succeeding payrolls were supported with complete documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly abide with the provisions of Section 23 of the Omnibus Rules on Leave. Also, we recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the HRMO to allow monetization of sick leave credits if and only if the employee applying for monetization has exhausted first his vacation leave credits. We further recommended that HRMO restate the vacation and sick leave credits of the employees who availed the monetization of both vacation and sick leave to properly reflect the correct balances of their leave credits.
observations.managementAction: The HRMO has already restated the balance of leave credits of the municipal officials and employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management stop issuing financial assistance directly to persons identified to be suffering from schizophrenia and similar conditions, but rather, identify guardians who are more competent to manage the assistance given to the client-beneficiary to ensure that the assistance will be used for its intended purpose.
observations.managementAction: No similar transaction was noted during period audited.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit to the Office of the Auditor a copy of perfected contract and its supporting documents within five (5) days following its perfection for review.
observations.managementAction: Succeeding contracts perfected were submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant maintain a separate cashbook and ledger, respectively for the DBP Savings Account 00-5-13530-545-3 to determine its actual balance in any given period. Further, we recommended that Municipal Accountant prepare monthly bank reconciliation statement for the said bank account.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer now maintains a cashbook for such savings account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Treasurer deposit intact all collections made by her and/or remitted to her by the collectors daily or not later than the next banking day, to prevent possible loss of government funds and/or misuse of the money held in her trust.
observations.managementAction: Succeeding collections were deposited intact.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer refrain from paying the salaries and wages of casual employees on a monthly rate instead use the daily rate as indicated in the plantilla of casual appointments, multiplied by the number of days that the casual employee actually worked and any applicable special non-working holiday/s, for the subsequent payrolls.
observations.managementAction: Casual employees were paid on a monthly rate as per LBC 115 dated January 03, 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the Management the following: (1) Require the concerned municipal employees to immediately refund the PIB received covering the CY 2017 and any subsequent disbursement, if any, for the same employee benefit; (2) Discontinue appropriating funds for the payment of PIB for the succeeding years to avoid audit disallowances; and (3) Grant Step Increment/s Due to Meritorious Performance instead of PIB to municipal employees who exemplify outstanding performances provided the provisions of CSC-DBM Joint Circular 1-2012 are met.
observations.managementAction: The employees concerned had refunded the total amount thru payroll deduction. Further, no appropriation for the succeeding year was provided for such employee benefit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: (1) stop the practice of allowing these Job Orders to attend trainings and seminars and claim the corresponding travelling allowances and per diems; (2) grant only the actual fare whenever they are directed to perform tasks outside their official station; and (3) require the Job Orders listed in Annexes C and D to refund immediately the claimed per diems, incidental expenses and travelling allowances on their respective travels.
observations.managementAction: Concerned employees hired under Job Order Contract had started refunding the amount. Total of P20,504.00 has been refunded.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 21.1 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 on the validity, accuracy and correctness of the entries published in the PhilGEPS website to avoid any possibility that prospective bidder/s submit an incorrect proposal.
observations.managementAction: No similar transaction noted during the period audited.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that BAC and TWG properly evaluate the qualifications of prospective suppliers to enable the agency to source the most qualified supplier to implement projects of the agency and to ensure that all procurements adhered with the requirements of the bidding.
observations.managementAction: No similar transaction noted during the period audited.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee Members faithfully observe and ensure that the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 are strictly complied with so as to avoid questioning the validity and legality of any procurement activity. Also, we recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer certify obligations only when there is a sufficient document evidencing the amount requested for obligation. Further, the Municipal Accountant to carefully examine the correctness and completeness of the supporting documents before effecting payment.
observations.managementAction: No similar transaction noted during the period audited.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers to support the balances appearing in the financial statements and regularly update the same so that verification and validation could easily be made.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant now maintains supporting schedules for Land, Land Improvements, Buildings and Several Inventory accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Bids and Awards Committee to require their suppliers, distributors, consultants and contractors to register with the PhilGEPS as prescribed under the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 in order for them to join in any procurement activities.
observations.managementAction: All suppliers of the LGU-Burgos were registered with PhilGEPS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the municipality to formulate a written policy guidelines in assessing the necessity of hiring casuals and job order employees taking into consideration the reasonable number needed by the different offices in order to avoid excessive workforce, idle man-hours and work duplication.
observations.managementAction: The management has already submitted the written policy guidelines in hiring job order employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to submit monthly accounts of the Municipality as prescribed under Section 6.05 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 in order to give time to the Auditor in the preparation of the Auditorial Contract Review Analysis Sheet of completed infrastructure projects for inspection and evaluation by a COA Technical Audit Specialist.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant consis-tently submits DVs beyond the prescribed time frame.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to examine the completeness of the supporting documents for every transactions of the Municipality before approving the same. If there are claims submitted to the Accounting Office for processing, but lacks the properly filled up required supporting documents, it shall be returned to the claimant for proper documentation.
observations.managementAction: These forms were properly filled up as to its standard form.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to see to it that all supporting documents attached to the disbursement vouchers are properly and completely accomplished in order to establish the validity of claims. Further, the Municipal Accountant must indicate in the pre and post-repair inspection report and in the equipment ledger card the nature and scope of the last repair done and other necessary information to all vehicles and equipment to ensure that same scope of work is not repeated.
observations.managementAction: Pre- and post-inspection reports were properly filled up as to its form.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Inventory Committee to conduct the required annual physical count of the PPE as of December 31 of every year and to submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the Auditor for verification.  Likewise, the prescribed records/ forms for PPE accounts should be maintained by the Property Officer and the Accounting Office to conform with the provisions on the Supply and Property Management in the LGUs.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Inventory Committee is yet to conduct inventory tagging of the properties.
observations.reason:
The Inventory Committee conducted physical count of PPE, though not completed, thus the non-submission of the RPCPPE within the prescribed period and non-reconciliation of the results with the accounting records. In addition, recorded PPE items were not completely supported with PPE Ledger Cards (PPELCs) in which the details of a specific PPE account can be gleaned. On the other hand, Property Cards were not maintained per category of PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the same. 

Reiterated in Observation No. 1, page 32 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly adhere to the above cited provisions of law in the handling of government funds. Collections must be deposited daily and intact or not later than the next banking day considering that the location of the depository bank is just few kilometers away from the Municipal Hall. 

Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer and ICO-Municipal Accountant should strictly follow the procedures in the recording of deposits to conform with the afore-cited rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | According to the Municipal Treasurer, deposit of daily collection is not feasible, though he will try to deposit at least twice a week.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer has not still been depositing his collections daily.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant require all the necessary documents for the purchase of the land to ascertain the legality, validity and propriety of the transaction.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | The management has appropriated funds for the processing of transfer.
observations.reason:
Management has not submitted any document necessary to support the purchase.

Reiterated in Observation No. 1, page 32 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management furnish the Auditor copies of perfected contracts and purchase orders together with all its supporting documents within five (5) days as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-001 for a timely contract review and in order that noted deficiencies therein could be immediately complied with.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Complied with the submission of contract for goods and services projects.
observations.reason:
Seventy-two perfected infrastructure contracts amounting to P320,388,030.58 and purchase orders together with their supporting documents were not submitted to the Auditor within five (5) days from execution and issuance thereof.

Reiterated in Observation No. 4, page 41 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU adhere to the provisions of the existing laws, rules and regulations regarding the payment of RATA of members of the local sanggunian and other municipal officials and employees.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Complied with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that disbursement vouchers are duly supported with the required basic documents to ensure their legality, validity and propriety.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | The Municipal Accountant shall ensure the completeness of supporting documents.
observations.reason:
Disbursements totaling to P11,207,434.97 were not supported with complete and proper documentation.

Reiterated in Observation No. 5, page 43 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LDRRM Council pass a resolution reverting the CYs 2011 and  2012  LDRRMF  unspent  balances  to  the  unappropriated  surplus  of  the  General Fund,  for adoption  by  the  Sangguniang Bayan. Further,  that   the   ICO-Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer prepare  the  necessary  transfer  of  funds  and  accounting  reports  to facilitate the said reversion and certify to the availability of said funds for subsequent reappropriation for social services.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | The unspent balances for calendar year 2011 and 2012 had already been reverted back to the General Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MDRRMO submit monthly Report on Source and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund on time in compliance with COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Report on Source and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund had been regularly submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate in the LGU’s Annual Budget one percent (1%) of its IRA for the LCPC and allocate the fund for programs, projects and activities for the protection of children.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Complied with the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the prompt implementation of all projects/ programs funded out of the tobacco excise tax in order to give the constituents, especially the tobacco farmers the early benefits that could be derived therefrom.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | The implementation of several projects are ongoing, though there are still projects that has no responsive bidders even after posting it several times on websites and conspicuous places. The agency will actively post the unimplemented projects to websites and conspicuous places to attract potential bidders.
observations.reason: There were still seventeen (17) unimplemented programs/ projects with a total amount of P100,825,000.00 from RA 7171 funds received in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management properly plan the implementation of programs/ projects/ activities so that the accomplishments and benefits that are expected to be derived therefrom are obtained/ achieved on time.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | The unimplemented project lacks technical personnel. The Municipal Engineering Office shall coordinate or source technical personnel that can help with implementation.
observations.reason: Included in the unimplemented amount in CY 2018 were savings from fully implemented projects which were not reappropriated in 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Chairperson of the GAD Focal Point System to fully implement programs/ projects/ activities funded under the GAD Fund in order to fully address gender issues/ problems of the constituents in the municipal level and to help the national government attain its goal in addressing gender issues in the country.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | The MSWDO mentioned that all programs are implemented, though the appropriated budget was not fully exhausted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality require the settlement of the disallowances of the two employees. Likewise, the ICO-Municipal Accountant should ensure that supporting documents of transactions are complete, proper and valid in order to prevent the same from being suspended/ disallowed in post-audit.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | The agency was able to settle the disallowances early 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:

Strictly adhere to the Accounting and Reporting Guidelines for the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund set forth under COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 20012 in which incurrence of expenses charged to the previous year’s LDRRMF should be recorded under the Trust Fund Book and closed with the Trust Liability-DRRMF Account at year-end; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entries.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 | Correcting entry recorded.
observations.reason: Correcting entry (JEV No. 100-2019-01-46 and 300-2019-01-01) were already recorded in the books of the LGU.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management:

Strengthen the controls on the handling and recording of PPEs by maintaining perpetual inventory records (Property Cards and  PPE Ledger Cards) and by performing periodic reconciliation of the said records and the actual inventory results; and

Investigate the causes for the missing PPE items and identify the persons deemed liable thereof.
