We reiterated to recommend the Local Chief Executive to issue an order/memorandum immediately for the conduct of physical inventory taking within a determined period of time and submit the inventory report within ten (10) days from the completion of the inventory taking as required under Chapter 7, Volume I of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for LGUs. Likewise, property ledger and property cards should be maintained by the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer. Further, we recommended that full time permanent personnel be employed in-charged of property records considering the properties of the municipality are increasing every year. Also, monitor the progress of the inventory taking to ensure the submission of prescribed and complete RPCPPE within the target period. For those PPEs found to be unserviceable, report them in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and dispose them in accordance with COA rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: Partially complied
observations.reason: The Municipal Mayor had designated a full time employee in-charge of property records. Also, such designated property officer has conducted the physical inventory taking, however, his records do not reconcile yet with the accounting records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers to support the balances appearing in the financial statements and regularly update the same so that verification and validation could easily be made.
observations.managementAction: Partially complied
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant now maintains supporting schedules for Land, Land Improvements, Buildings and Several Inventory accounts, however, these records do not reconcile with the Designated Property Officer’s records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the provisions of DPWH Department Order No. 176 dated November 23, 2015 in order to present fairly the financial statements of the municipality at year-end.
observations.managementAction: Complied
observations.reason: Infrastructure Assets account were already provided with depreciation expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that programs, projects and activities under the Priority Development Projects for the Barangays of the 20% Economic Development Fund and RA 7171 be specified / identified for proper planning and budgeting. Further, if the project is described as generic terms, a prior approval or authorization of the sanggunian is required.
observations.managementAction: Complied

observations.recommendation: We recommended the members of the BAC to strictly adhere to the policies and procedures set forth in RA 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations and should be guided by the rules set in COA Circular No 76-41 dated July 30, 1976. The Municipal Accountant is further encouraged to exercise more diligence in reviewing the necessary documents before endorsing the same for payment.
observations.managementAction: Complied
observations.reason: There were no procurements with adjacent dates using the Small Value Procure-ment Method were noted during the Calendar Year 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Bids and Awards Committee to require their suppliers, distributors, consultants and contractors to register with the PhilGEPS as prescribed under the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 in order for them to join in any procurement activities.
observations.managementAction: Partially complied
observations.reason: There were still suppliers that are not registered with PhilGEPS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant/ Treasurer to stop the practice of paying PERA to casual employees on a monthly rate of P2,000.00, instead use the rate of P90.91 per day as required under Item 5.5 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2009-3 dated August 18, 2009 to avoid disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: Complied
observations.reason: The Accounting Office now uses the daily rate of P90.91 in computing the PERA for casual employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the municipality to formulate a written policy guidelines in assessing the necessity of hiring casuals and job order employees taking into consideration the reasonable number needed by the different offices in order to avoid excessive workforce, idle man-hours and work duplication.
observations.managementAction: No management action
observations.reason: The management has not yet submitted the written policy guidelines in hiring job order employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended management to direct the Municipal Accountant and the MSWDO to submit immediately the required supporting documents and instruct the processing employees to exercise utmost care in the review of documents to ensure its completeness and validity of the transactions.
observations.managementAction: Complied
observations.reason: Distribution reports, menus, and photos during the actual feeding program were already submitted to the Office of the Auditor for evaluation. Succeeding SFPs were duly supported with the complete documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to submit monthly accounts of the Municipality as prescribed under Section 6.05 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 in order to give time to the Auditor in the preparation of the Auditorial Contract Review Analysis Sheet of completed infrastructure projects for inspection and evaluation by a COA Technical Audit Specialist.
observations.managementAction: No management action
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant consis-tently submits DVs for infrastructure projects beyond the prescribed time frame.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere on the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System specifically COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 in order to have a fair presentation of the account Infrastructure Assets in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Partially complied
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant pro-perly disclosed the Road Networks in the Notes to Financial Statements, however, these pertains only to the municipal roads constructed from 2016 to 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to be guided by Section 31, Chapter 2 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for LGUs, Vol. II in order to have a basis for recording in the appropriate journals.
observations.managementAction: Complied
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant now prepares JEVs for collections and payrolls as basis for recording in the appropriate journals.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the municipality to observe the minimum employee’s monthly net take home pay prescribed under Section 47 of the General Appropriations Act of FY 2017 in order that the efficiency and effectiveness of employees in the performance of their duties and functions could not be affected.
observations.managementAction: Complied
observations.reason: The management now faithfully complies with the mandatory minimum net take home pay for each employee.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to examine the completeness of the supporting documents for every transactions of the Municipality before approving the same. If there are claims submitted to the Accounting Office for processing, but lacks the properly filled up required supporting documents, it shall be returned to the claimant for proper documentation.
observations.managementAction: Partially complied
observations.reason: Several purchase orders were still not properly filled up as to its standard form.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to affix her signature on the funds available portion of the Purchase Order as required under Section 28 of the General Appropriations Act for 2017 in order that funds have been duly appropriated and available for expenditures.
observations.managementAction: Complied
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant now affixes her signature in the “Funds Available” portion of the Purchase Orders.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to see to it that all supporting documents attached to the disbursement vouchers are properly and completely accomplished in order to establish the validity of claims. Further, the Municipal Accountant must indicate in the pre and post-repair inspection report and in the equipment ledger card the nature and scope of the last repair done and other necessary information to all vehicles and equipment to ensure that same scope of work is not repeated.
observations.managementAction: Partially complied
observations.reason: Several Pre- and post-inspection reports were still not properly filled up as to its form.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the 20% Economic Development Fund be fully utilized during the year to address the priority needs of the people and to attain maximum benefits for the intended recipients. We further recommended that the Municipality must strictly adhere to the provisions of   the 20% Economic Development Fund.
observations.managementAction: No management action
observations.reason: The upgrading of Pagali-Ablan-Saoit Water System under 20% Economic Development Fund has not yet started during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Treasurer prepare the Certified List of Real Property Taxpayers with the corresponding amount of RPT/SET due and collectible each year as basis in setting up the correct RPT/SET Receivables by the Municipal Accountant. The Municipal Accountant make proper coordination with the Municipal Treasurer and secure copies of the accomplished identified taxpayer cards as basis in posting the RPT/SET Receivable accounts to ensure that the respective accounts are accurately presented in the financial statements. Further, the Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjustments to arrive at a reliable and correct balance of the accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting Municipal Assessor to obtain Transfer Certificate of Title of all the parcels of land acquired by the Municipality as required under Section 449, Volume I of GAAM and Section 39(2) of PD 1445 to serve as proof of absolute ownership over these properties.  Moreover, the Municipal Mayor should instruct the Acting Municipal Assessor and the Municipal Treasurer to cause for the transfer from the name of the seller to the Municipality of Burgos of the Tax Declaration of the two parcels of land acquired in the years 1999 and 2013 with area of 4,271 square meters and 139,036 square meters respectively.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken yet because no fund was appropriated for this purpose.

The request shall be supported by complete documents enumerated in Section 8.3 of COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: No records available.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer settle his cash advance for payroll within five (5) days after each fifteen (15) day/end of the month pay period.
observations.reason: Cash advance was decreased by P15,398.27.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Engineer facilitate the posting of signboards consisting all on-going PPAs of the Municipality and the posting of tarpaulin signboards for infrastructure projects in their correct specifications at the venue where the PPAs is located or carried out. This will enable the public to have knowledge regarding the relevant information of the PPA which promotes good governance through transparency. It also enables them to assess whether the PPAs were completed justifiably as to contract amount and duration.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer intensify the collection of Real Property and Special Education Taxes.  Notices of collection should be sent to the delinquent taxpayers to remind them of their obligation to pay their taxes. Likewise, conduct massive tax campaign in all the barangays and offer tax amnesty in order to motivate delinquent taxpayers to avail of the offer.  In addition, seek the cooperation of the barangay officials, anyway, the barangays have also shares in the collection of real property taxes.
observations.managementAction: Conducted tax campaign program in barangays.
observations.reason: There was a decrease in  collection efficiency by 9.08%.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to see to it that the plans and specifications are strictly followed in the implementation of infrastructure projects in order to deliver to the tobacco farmers-constituents the best quality projects they rightfully deserve.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the prompt implementation of all funds received from other national government agencies in order to deliver on time the benefits due to the intended beneficiaries and to avoid returning the unutilized funds to the donor agencies.
observations.managementAction: Project implementation is on-going.
observations.reason: Implemen-tation of the projects are still on-going.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management should issue immediately to the end-users succeeding purchases of this nature in order for the latter to be benefited from the timely use of the purchased equipment/ machinery.

We recommended and  the Municipal Accountant agreed to strictly comply with PPSAS 17 and the corresponding PAG through the assignment of residual values for every item of PPE for enhanced accountability and prevent derecognition prior to actual disposal.
observations.managementAction: Depreciation and Lapsing Schedule for 2018 was prepared with residual values compliant to PAG 6 of the PPSAS 17.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed to immediately submit detailed justification including the effects on cost and economic justification to support the changes that are desirable in the design standards or principal features of the completed project in accordance with the revised IRR of RA 9184. 

We recommended further that Management henceforth refrain from allowing major modifications in the scope of work and relevant features of bidded projects as it may be taken as nullifying the effect of public bidding and may result in manifest injustice to other bidders.
observations.managementAction: Justifications were already submitted to COA last March 21, 2018 thru a letter-reply dated March 13, 2018 to Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) 2018-06.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Engineer agreed to:

Always require posting of warranty security in any acceptable form for completed infrastructure projects in accordance with Section 62 of  the 2016 IRR of RA 9184; 
Henceforth establish a system and maintain records on specific warranty periods and security for each procurement of civil works.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inspectorate Team to conduct inspection of completed projects after the one (1) year defects liability period and issue the corresponding Certificate of Final Acceptance to the contractors if no defect/ deficiency was detected.
observations.managementAction: As per management, the Engineering office already started maintaining records on warranty periods and issuing Certificates of Final Acceptance to contractors.

observations.recommendation:
While we recognize the on-going efforts of the municipality for the operation and maintenance of the SALINTUBIG projects, we recommended that Management:

Require the WATSAN council to prepare a reasonable work plan for the  consideration of the Sangguniang Bayan in the provision of annual funding;
Either assist the community to  establish the WSPs through capacity building activities for eventual turn-over or undertake ring-fencing; and
Monitor and evaluate the physical and financial aspects of operations including its functionality, effectiveness and efficiency for the benefit of the intended end-users.
observations.managementAction:
The mayor’s Office has issued Executive Order No. 2017-09 creating the WATSAN Council. The SB has passed an ordinance regarding the operation of the water system and was already approved by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan.

Operations of the water system was already included in the 2019 budget.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to ensure availability of resources and inclusion of the lacking items in the formulation of the LDRRMFIP in  compliance with the minimum preparedness standards set forth under the NDPP not only to support the initiatives of the DILG but most significantly to avert the loss of lives during disasters.
observations.managementAction:
Additional disaster-related equipment were purchase. HASAR and WASAR Trainings were conducted for CY 2018.
The association of volunteers was organized and registered.

Based on the 2018 Annual Budget of the Municipality, there’s one percent for the mandatory appropriation for Local Council for the Protection of Children.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit his collections regularly and intact as prescribed under Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 to avoid the accumulation of cash on hand and the exposure of the same to possible risk of loss and misappropriation. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant ensure the monthly conduct of reconciliation of her Cash-Local Treasury Ledger balances with that of the Cashbook-Collections of the Municipal Treasurer and submit a Statement of Reconciliation to the Audit Team every given date.
observations.managementAction: CY2017 AAR, Observation No. 1, pages 37-39

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to do the following:

For Annex A and D - Status of Unliquidated Cash Advance and Status of Other Receivables, respectively,

avail all remedies in the identification of the Accountable Officers and demand immediate settlement of all unliquidated cash advances from officials and employees concerned following the above cited regulations.

if warranted, withhold the salaries of those who persistently fail or refuse to comply despite repeated demand for liquidation and/or file appropriate administrative and criminal charges against them to compel liquidation.

ensure that the officers and employees with outstanding or unliquidated cash advances should not be granted additional cash advances, until the previous ones are settled, to preclude accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.

ensure that all regulations pertaining to cash advances should be strictly enforced.

monitor the outstanding cash advances of officials and employees and demand immediate settlement after its purpose has been served.

For Annex B and C - Status of Fund Transfers to Other Government/Implementing Agencies (IAs) and Status of Fund Transfers to Non-Governmental Organizations/People's Organizations (NGOs/POs), respectively, exhaust all remedies in the identification of the National Government Agencies of which the amount of P297,416.25 was granted. Issue demand letters for settlement without prejudice to the remedies cited in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 31, 2016 which illustrates the Guidelines and Procedures on the Write-Off of Dormant Receivable Accounts, Fund Transfers to NGAs, LGUs, and GOCCs.

For Cash advances which were disbursed without appropriation and were entrusted to the MPDC but not yet liquidated as of year-end amounting to P35,000.00 and P28,560.00 respectively, require the Accountable Officer/s to refund immediately the amounts in compliance with Section 5.7 of the COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Disallowances, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: CY2017 AAR, Observation No. 4, pages 46-51

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management submit to COA a request for the write-off of the Due from GOCCs that remained dormant for more than 10 years, after satisfying the documentary requirements cited in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: CY2017 AAR, Observation No. 6, pages 53-54

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management do the following:

require the Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of local roads under their jurisdiction and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Local Road Network at the end of every accounting period.

pursue complete reconciliation of the RPCLRN and the updated Registry of Public Infrastructures in coordination with the General Services Officer and the Accountant through detailed review of prior year’s transactions in order to identify any unrecorded road networks.


direct the Municipal Accountant to: 

(a) maintain Local Road Network Ledger Card and maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for the road and its components namely: road lot, road pavement, drainage and slope protection structures, and other miscellaneous structures; 

(b) support the beginning balance of Road Networks and its components amounting to P37,194,971.09 in the GL and Ledger Cards with the JEV and working papers; 

(c) make details of the recorded road, highways and bridges which were closed to government equity in the prior years and subsequently support the recording in its books of accounts the amount of road works equivalent to the required transition provision as of CY 2017, and

(d) make a disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements as illustrated in Annex B of said circular. 

instruct the General Services Officer to (a) complete Local Road Inventory records with Road Map and Local Road Network Property Cards; and (b) submit the  Report on Local Road Network of the LGU prepared by the Inventory Committee as shown in Annex A of the circular.

observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this report

We recommended and Management agreed to immediately designate an employee who shall be responsible person to facilitate the registration and insurance of all motor vehicles owned by the Municipality. Apply and/or renew the registration of all serviceable motor vehicles of the municipality with LTO and contracts of insurance with the GSIS to avoid additional expenses in terms of penalties and payment of damages to third party liabilities in case of vehicular accidents.

We recommended and Management agreed to allocate funds and designate or assign a focal person/official responsible to facilitate the processing of the application for land title with the Land Registration Authority. Necessary actions should be taken to transfer the title of the lot under the name of the LGU to establish the validity and propriety on the acquisition and to avoid the risk of facing future legal issues which may cause additional expense to the government.

We recommended and Management agreed to: a. require the Solid Waste Management Board to perform its functions in accordance with RA 9003; b. strictly implement the Segregation At Source and Segregated Collection to all barangays; c. submit the safety closure and rehabilitation plan of the open dumpsite to the EMB Regional Office for review and evaluation; d. provide sufficient funding not only from local sources of the LGU but also secure grants from other government agencies; and e. enforce the collection system on fines and penalties of violators.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management ensure that all disbursements of the municipality be supported with complete documentation. It is emphasized that complete documentation does not only mean the submission of documents but also the completeness and consistency of the details thereof. 

In this regard, the lacking and explanatory documents, if any, should be submitted to ascertain the validity and propriety of the disbursements totaling P470,160.00. Otherwise, the same may be suspended or disallowed in audit.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated to recommend the Local Chief Executive to issue an order/memorandum immediately for the conduct of physical inventory taking within a determined period of time and submit the inventory report within ten (10) days from the completion of the inventory taking as required under Chapter 7, Volume I of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for LGUs. Likewise, property ledger and property cards be maintained by the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer. Further, we recommended that a full time permanent personnel be employed in-charged of property records considering the properties of the municipality is increasing every year. Also, monitor the progress of the inventory taking to ensure the submission of prescribed and complete RPCPPE within the target period. For those PPEs found to be unserviceable, report them in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and dispose them in accordance with COA rules and regulations.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers to support the balances appearing in the financial statements and regularly update the same so that verification and validation could easily be made.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the provisions of DPWH Department Order No. 176 dated November 23, 2015 in order to present fairly the financial statements of the municipality at year-end.

We recommended the Acting Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact daily all collections with authorized depository bank as prescribed by the provisions of Section 169 of PD 1445 and Section 32 of the Manual on the , for LGUs Vol. to prevent and avoid possible misuse, misappropriation or even loss of government funds.

We recommended that programs, projects and activities under the Priority Development Projects for the Barangays of the 20% Economic Development Fund and RA 7171 be specified/identified for proper planning and budgeting. Further, if the project is described as generic terms, a prior approval or authorization of the sanggunian is required.

We recommended the members of the BAC to strictly adhere to the policies and procedures set forth in RA 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations and should be guided by the rules set in COA Circular No 76-41 dated July 30, 1976. The Municipal Accountant is further encouraged to exercise more diligence in reviewing the necessary documents before endorsing the same for payment.

We recommended the Bids and Awards Committee to require their suppliers, distributors, consultants and contractors to register with the PhilGEPS as prescribed under the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 in order for them to join in any procurement activities.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant/Treasurer to stop the practice of paying PERA to casual employees on a monthly rate of P2,000.00, instead use the rate of P90.91 per day as required under Item 5.5 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2009-3 dated August 18, 2009 to avoid disallowance in audit.

We recommended the municipality to formulate a written policy guidelines in assessing the necessity of hiring casuals and job order employees taking into consideration the reasonable number needed by the different offices in order to avoid excessive workforce, idle manhours and work duplication.

We recommended management to direct the Municipal Accountant and the MSWDO to submit immediately the required supporting documents and instruct the processing employees to exercise utmost care in the review of documents to ensure its completeness and validity of the transactions.

We recommended that only payrolls with sufficient supporting documents be processed for payment by the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer in order to prove that services had been rendered by the employee.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to submit monthly accounts of the Municipality as prescribed under Section 6.05 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 in order to give time to the Auditor in the preparation of the Auditorial Contract Review Analysis Sheet of completed infrastructure projects for inspection and evaluation by a COA Technical Audit Specialist.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere on the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System specifically COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 in order to have a fair presentation of the account Infrastructure Assets in the financial statements.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to be guided by Section 31, Chapter 2 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for LGUs, Vol. II in order to have a basis for recording in the appropriate journals.

We recommended the municipality to observe the minimum employee’s monthly net take home pay prescribed under Section 47 of the General Appropriations Act of FY 2017 in order that the efficiency and effectiveness of employees in the performance of  their duties and functions could not be affected.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to examine the completeness of the supporting documents for every transactions of the Municipality before approving the same. If there are claims submitted to the Accounting Office for processing, but lacks the properly filled up required supporting documents, it shall be returned to the claimant for proper documentation.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to affix her signature on the funds available portion of the Purchase Order as required under Section 28 of the General Appropriations Act for 2017 in order that funds have been duly appropriated and available for expenditures.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to see to it that all supporting documents attached to the disbursement vouchers be properly and completely accomplished in order to establish the validity of claims. Further, the Municipal Accountant must indicate in the pre and post-repair inspection report and in the equipment ledger card the nature and scope of the last repair done and other necessary information to all vehicles and equipment to ensure that same scope of work is not repeated.

We recommended that the 20% Economic Development Fund be fully utilized during the year to address the priority needs of the people and to attain maximum benefits for the intended recipients. We further recommended that the Municipality must  strictly adhere to the provisions of   the 20% Economic Development Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting Municipal Assessor to obtain Transfer Certificate of Title of all the parcels of land acquired by the Municipality as required under Section 449, Volume I of GAAM and Section 39(2) of PD 1445 to serve as proof of absolute ownership over these properties.  Moreover, the Municipal Mayor should instruct the Acting Municipal Assessor and the Municipal Treasurer to cause for the transfer from the name of the seller to the Municipality of Burgos of the Tax Declaration of the two parcels of land acquired in the years 1999 and 2013 with area of 4,271 square meters and 139,036 square meters respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor  require the Municipal Treasurer   to deposit collections intact with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day.  The amount collected for the day should be the amount to be deposited in order not to expose the funds for possible misuse or misappropriation and at the same time this will enable the Municipality to earn additional income in the form of interest on bank deposits.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Accountant exert extra effort to locate records of these receivables.  If such efforts exerted are futile, the Municipal Mayor may file the request for authority to write –off said accounts except the accounts Receivables – Disallowances/Charges and Other Receivables arising from cash shortage, to the COA Audit Team Leader and/or Supervising Auditor.  The request shall be supported by complete documents enumerated in Section 8.3 of COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer settle his cash advance for payroll within five (5) days after each fifteen (15) day/end of the month pay period.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman and the Municipal Engineer require the BAC Secretariat to furnish the Auditor a copy of the contracts and all supporting documents within five (5) working days after execution thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer facilitate the posting of signboards consisting all on-going PPAs of the Municipality and the posting of tarpaulin signboards for infrastructure projects in their correct specifications at the venue where the PPAs is located or carried out. This will enable the public to have knowledge regarding the relevant information of the PPA which promotes good governance through transparency. It also enables them to assess whether the PPAs were completed justifiably as to contract amount and duration.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer intensify the collection of Real Property and Special Education Taxes.  Notices of collection should be sent to the delinquent taxpayers to remind them of their obligation to pay their taxes. Likewise, conduct massive tax campaign in all the barangays and offer tax amnesty in order to motivate delinquent taxpayers to avail of the offer.  In addition, seek the cooperation of the barangay officials, anyway, the barangays have also shares in the collection of real property taxes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the prompt implementation of all projects/programs funded out of the tobacco excise taxes  in order to give the tobacco farmers who worked hard for the generation of the funds the benefits they rightfully deserve.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to see to it that the plans and specifications are strictly followed in the implementation of infrastructure projects in order to deliver to the tobacco farmers-constituents the best quality projects they rightfully deserve.
We recommended that the municipal officials and employees concerned implement fully all the programs/ projects / activities embodied under the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the prompt implementation of all funds received from other national government agencies in order to deliver on time the benefits due to the intended beneficiaries and to avoid returning the unutilized funds to the donor agencies.

We recommended that management should issue immediately to the end-users succeeding purchases of this nature in order for the latter to be benefited from the timely use of the purchased equipment/machinery.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Chairperson of the LCPC to fully implement programs/projects funded under the one percent of the internal revenue allotment geared towards the promotion and protection of interest and welfare of children in the country.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Chairperson of the GAD Focal Point System to fully implement programs/projects funded under the GAD Plan in order to fully address gender issues/problems of the constituents in the municipal level and to help the national government attain its goal of addressing gender issues in the country.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting Municipal Accountant to see to it that all disbursements are supported with valid and complete documents. Further, management should adhere to Sections 9.4 and 10.4 of COA Circular 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009 regarding the settlement of suspensions and disallowances.  Audit suspensions should be settled within ninety (90) calendar days from receipt of the NS to avoid maturity of these suspensions to disallowances and disallowances should be settled within six (6) months from receipt of the ND by the persons liable.

We recommended and  the Municipal Accountant agreed to strictly comply with PPSAS 17 and the corresponding PAG through the assignment of residual values for every item of PPE for enhanced accountability and prevent derecognition prior to actual disposal.

We recommended and the Management agreed to immediately submit detailed justification including the effects on cost and economic justification to support the changes that are desirable in the design standards or principal features of the completed project in accordance with the revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended further that Management henceforth refrain from allowing major modifications in the scope of work and relevant features of bidded projects as it may be taken as nullifying the effect of public bidding and may result in manifest injustice to other bidders.

We recommended and the Municipal Engineer agreed to:
Always require posting of warranty security in any acceptable form for completed infrastructure projects in accordance with Section 62 of  the 2016 IRR of RA 9184;
Henceforth establish a system and maintain records on specific warranty periods and security for each procurement of civil works.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inspectorate Team to conduct inspection of completed projects after the one (1) year defects liability period and issue the corresponding Certificate of Final Acceptance to the contractors if no defect /deficiency was detected.

Immediately submit the documents pertaining to one (1) contract identified in Appendix A; and
Henceforth forward to the Audit Team all perfected contracts and Purchase Orders along with all the required attachments within five (5) days from perfection as provided under COA Circular No. 2009-001.  This will enable the Audit Team to conduct timely evaluation of contracts and review of items purchased and thereafter immediately communicate to management any defects/deficiencies noted thereon.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure availability of resources and inclusion of the lacking items in the formulation of the LDRRMFIP in  compliance with the minimum preparedness standards set forth under the NDPP not only to support the initiatives of the DILG but most significantly to avert the loss of lives during disasters.

Wecommended the Inventory Committee for their effort in the conduct of
annual physical count of all properties of the LGU and recommended the
Committee to conduct the same yearly and prepare and submit the RPCPPE in
accordance with Section 124, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs.

Likewise, require the Property Custodian and the Municipal Accountant to
maintain complete and updated property and subsidiary records in conformity with
Sections 119 and 120, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs.

Wealso recommended the immediate incorporation of the noted adjustments
both in the RPCPPE and Accounting records to finally reconcile the balances.

We recommended that management dispose the unserviceable properties of
the agency in accordance with Sections 76 and 79 of PD 1445. Disposal thereof may:
1. generate additional funds, when sold, to be used for the agency’s operations;
2. relieve the responsible official of accountability over said properties;
3. prevent said unserviceable properties from further deterioration and loss of
value; and
4. allow the agency to use the space being occupied by these unserviceable
properties.

We recommended that management request for write-off of the dormant
accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016
in order to reconcile and clear the books of accounts of dormant receivable accounts
and fund transfers for fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended thatthe Local Chief Executiveinstruct the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the balances of fund transfers
in order to effect the immediate return of the unexpended balances of the same to
the concerned source agencies pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013 dated
December 13, 1994.

We recommended that management strictly follow the provisions of Revenue
Regulations No. 2-98 as amended by Revenue Regulations No. 10-2008 in
withholding of taxes on all compensations being received by the officials and
employees of the Municipality, including honoraria, and remit the same to BIR in
accordance with said regulations.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the following
documents for the payment of BAC Honoraria for us to evaluate the same, to wit:
1. Office Order creating and designating the BAC composition and
authorizing the members to collect honoraria;
2. Minutes of BAC Meeting;
3. Notice of award to the winning bidder of procurement activity being
claimed;
4. Certification that the procurement involves competitive bidding;
5. Attendance sheet listing names of attendees to the BAC meeting.

We recommended that management strictly adhere with the provisions of
Executive Order No. 248 dated May 29, 1995 and CSC Resolution No. 020790dated
June 5, 2002. Likewise, we recommended that management stop granting per diems
and incidental expenses to job order personnel but allow them to claim actual fare if
for the benefit of the agency that require them to travel outside their official station.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer continue sending demand
letters and notice of revocation of lease contract, as the case maybe, to delinquent
stall holders to remind them to pay promptly their monthly rentals. Likewise, we
have recommended the Treasury Office to observe strict and full enforcement of the
Market Code of the Municipality to create more funds for other beneficial projects.

We recommended that Management:
 require the Municipal Treasurer to continue and intensify/improve their
collection strategies on taxes due and collectible by sending RPTOP’s and
notice of delinquency to taxpayers and forming committee to conduct an
information dissemination regarding the importance of paying taxes promptly
and the consequences of non-payment of taxes and
 if circumstances warrant, impose the provisions of Sections 258 and 260 of the
Local Government Code of 1991 in the collections of delinquent taxes to satisfy
taxes, penalties and costs with the following:
1. Seizure of personal property of the taxpayer and sale thereof at public
auction;
2. File civil suit with the proper court; and
3. Levy on real property and sale of delinquent property or a usable
portion thereof at public auction.

We recommendedthat the Municipal Treasurer increase the amount
withheld from the salaries of the delinquent employees to settle the loans within this
year since these were already long outstanding. Likewise, we have recommended to
continue sending demand letters and employ aggressive collection strategies to
enforce collection of the loans especially to the borrowers related to the employees of
the LGU. If all means failed, resort to legal action in the collection of overdue
receivables to avoid possible loss of government funds.

We recommended that management require theimmediate submission of the
lacking documents to the audit team for verification and audit purposes.We also
recommended that Section 4 (6) of PD 1445, Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-
001 and provisions of RA 9184 be strictly followedto establish validity of the claim.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive monitor the
implementation of the construction of sanitary landfill that will meet the criteria
provided under Section 41 of RA 9003 to prevent leachate and hazardous waste that
would contaminate the environment and health of the people.

Likewise, we recommended that the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan
of the Municipality be prepared in accordance with the standards set by the
Municipal Solid Waste Board and be submitted for approval in compliance to
Section 16 of RA 9003.

We recommended that the Municipality continue to appropriate at least five
per cent fund requirement in the annual budget proposal and to incorporate only
GAD-related programs and activities in the GAD Plan to address gender issues and
concerns affecting the welfare and full development of women in order to eradicate
gender-based inequalities and enable women and men to equally contribute and
benefit from development. Likewise, we recommended the immediate submission
of the GAD Accomplishment Report in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-
001 dated March 18, 2014.

We recommended that the Municipality appropriate one percent of the
Internal Revenue Allotment for the implementation of LCPC programs, projects
and activities as enumerated under Section II of DILG Memorandum Circular No.
2012-120 to fully address the prevention of juvenile delinquency and welfare of the
children. Likewise, we have recommended management to prepare its Annual
LCPC Plan and Budget and Report of Accomplishments and Utilization and to
submit the same to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Accountant to prepare and submit through the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Officer the Monthly Report of Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF
on or before the 15th day after the end of each month in compliance with COA
Circular 2012-002.

We recommended that management continue to collect/deduct and remit
taxes due to BIR. Likewise, the ending balance of P9,294.57 as of December 31,
2017 be remitted to avoid surcharges/penalties.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit his collections regularly and intact as prescribed under Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 to avoid the accumulation of cash on hand and the exposure of the same to possible risk of loss and misappropriation. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant ensure the monthly conduct of reconciliation of her Cash-Local Treasury Ledger balances with that of the Cashbook-Collections of the Municipal Treasurer and submit a Statement of Reconciliation to the Audit Team every given date.

We recommended that Management require the concerned offices to effect reconciling items of the cash balances. Assign personnel in each office to make monthly reconciliation of accounts prior to the preparation of the financial statements and bank reconciliation statements on a regular basis to refrain from submission of the required reports beyond the deadline period and accumulation of reconciling items.
For the outstanding checks that are already stale, these should be cancelled after exhaustion of all remedies such as written notices to the payees.  After which the Municipal Treasurer issue of a new check upon presentation/surrender of the cancelled stale check and the corresponding DV and supporting documents be returned by the Municipal Treasurer to the Municipal Accountant. Further, the corresponding amounts cancelled stale checks should be reverted back to the book balances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to do the following:
For Annex A and D - Status of Unliquidated Cash Advance and Status of Other Receivables, respectively,
avail all remedies in the identification of the Accountable Officers and demand immediate settlement of all unliquidated cash advances from officials and employees concerned following the above cited regulations.
if warranted, withhold the salaries of those who persistently fail or refuse to comply despite repeated demand for liquidation and/or file appropriate administrative and criminal charges against them to compel liquidation.
ensure that the officers and employees with outstanding or unliquidated cash advances should not be granted additional cash advances, until the previous ones are settled, to preclude accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.
ensure that all regulations pertaining to cash advances should be strictly enforced.
monitor the outstanding cash advances of officials and employees and demand immediate settlement after its purpose has been served.
For Annex B and C - Status of Fund Transfers to Other Government/Implementing Agencies (IAs) and Status of Fund Transfers to Non-Governmental Organizations/People's Organizations (NGOs/POs), respectively, exhaust all remedies in the identification of the National Government Agencies of which the amount of P297,416.25 was granted. Issue demand letters for settlement without prejudice to the remedies cited in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 31, 2016 which illustrates the Guidelines and Procedures on the Write-Off of Dormant Receivable Accounts, Fund Transfers to NGAs, LGUs, and GOCCs.
For Cash advances which were disbursed without appropriation and were entrusted to the MPDC but not yet liquidated as of year-end amounting to P35,000.00 and P28,560.00 respectively, require the Accountable Officer/s to refund immediately the amounts in compliance with Section 5.7 of the COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Disallowances, if warranted.

We recommended that   the Management through the Municipal Accountant expedite the request for write-off to the Commission on Audit the account balances which were considered dormant and could no longer be collected pursuant to Section 10 of COA Circular No. 97-001.

We recommended that the Management submit to COA a request for the write-off of the Due from GOCCs that remained dormant for more than 10 years, after satisfying the documentary requirements cited in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Inventory Committee to fast track the comprehensive physical count of all properties of the Municipality and submit the Report of the Physical Inventory of PPE to Commission on Audit pursuant to Sec. 124 of the NGAs Manual to establish the correct value of its assets.

We recommended that the Management do the following:
require the Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of local roads under their jurisdiction and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Local Road Network at the end of every accounting period.
pursue complete reconciliation of the RPCLRN and the updated Registry of Public Infrastructures in coordination with the General Services Officer and the Accountant through detailed review of prior year’s transactions in order to identify any unrecorded road networks.
direct the Municipal Accountant to:
(a) maintain Local Road Network Ledger Card and maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for the road and its components namely: road lot, road pavement, drainage and slope protection structures, and other miscellaneous structures;
(b) support the beginning balance of Road Networks and its components amounting to P37,194,971.09 in the GL and Ledger Cards with the JEV and working papers;
(c) make details of the recorded road, highways and bridges which were closed to government equity in the prior years and subsequently support the recording in its books of accounts the amount of road works equivalent to the required transition provision as of CY 2017, and
(d) make a disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements as illustrated in Annex B of said circular.
instruct the General Services Officer to (a) complete Local Road Inventory records with Road Map and Local Road Network Property Cards; and (b) submit the  Report on Local Road Network of the LGU prepared by the Inventory Committee as shown in Annex A of the circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough review and analysis of all outstanding accounts payable and if found to have no valid claims against the local government unit, cause the reversion of the dormant and long outstanding payables to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund to ensure that only valid claims remain in the books and a more reliable presentation in accounting.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue to reconcile the LGU’s records with that of the Provincial Government of Isabela to establish the correct balances of the reciprocal accounts.  Likewise, provide the audit team a statement of reconciliation showing that the two books have reconciled as of given date.

We recommended that the Municipality refund the unused or excess balances of the funds received to the source agencies by drawing a check payable to BTr/concerned agency/ies especially those which are already undertaken pursuant to Items 4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 in order the amount to be refunded and could be used for other projects/programs of the National Government and to relieve the Municipality from any accountability/liability for the said unused funds.

We recommended that the Management do the following:
instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit a copy of liquidation report and refund the unutilized balance of the fund in compliance with Section 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.
ensure that future fund transfers be fully implemented within the required time frame to avoid non-usage of fund.  Henceforth, see to it that the COA Audit Team is always furnished a copy of duly notarized and approved MOA, if any.

We recommended that the Management make the actual cash transfer of the current year’s unspent LDRRMF’s balance of the 30% Quick Response Fund and the DRRMF-MOOE, except Capital Outlay, to the trust liability bank account amounting to P2,235,401.05.  Likewise, ensure the transfer of succeeding years unspent DRRMF balances at the end of the year pursuant to Annex C of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF within the period prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002, which is on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.

We recommended that the Municipality employ effective measures to expedite the implementation of the projects to provide beneficiaries of the early/optimum utilization of the program.  Likewise, prioritize and fast track the implementation of fully funded projects to ensure their completion on its target set, thus precluding unwarranted delay of the implementation of programs, projects and activities.

We recommended  that   the Management strictly comply with the DILG MC No. 2012-120   especially on the budgetary requirements of 1% allocation from the IRA to strengthen   its   Local   Councils   for   the Protection   of   Children   as stipulated in Section 15 of R.A. 9344 and to support the implementation of the programs, projects and activities of the latter for welfare of the nation’s future capital assets.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant comply with the rules and regulations on the submission of the disbursement vouchers together with the supporting documents, Official Receipts, Monthly Trial Balances and Bank Reconciliation Statements to avoid suspensions in audit.

We recommended that the MPDC- then OIC/Municipal Accountant submit   the  lacking   and   necessary   corrective/explanatory  documents for verification, for the transactions namely: (1) Recirculating Dryer with Biomass Furnace amounting to P1,347,343.75; (2) Beauty & Massage Therapy amounting to P351,110.00; and (3) Meat and Fish Processing amounting to P470,160.00 otherwise, the transaction may be suspended or disallowed in audit. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant to ascertain the validity and propriety of the disbursements based on the documents to be submitted by the MPDC prior to submission to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the collectors/accountable officers submit monthly the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) to the Municipal Treasurer. Further, upon submission by the collectors/accountable officers of their individual reports (RAAF), the Municipal Treasurer shall consolidate the reports in the Consolidated Report  of Accountability for Accountable Forms (CRAAF) and submit these reports to the Office of the Auditor not later than the fifth day of the following month.
Likewise, we recommended submission of CRAAF with every individual Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) for all collectors/accountable officers in full starting January 2017 onwards and strictly comply with the requirement on inclusion of checks in all funds as among the accountable forms reported in the RAAF/CRAAF.

We recommended  that   Management   ensure the liquidations for RATA are   duly supported with complete documentation as required by the existing rules and regulations so that the validity and accuracy of the disbursements could be determined. Submit the required lacking documents for the payments of RATA for January to April 2017 amounting to P346,858.32 to avoid issuance of Notice of Suspension. Also, explain the inconsistencies of documents submitted.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer take remedial measures/action on all obsolete/spoiled accountable forms (Checks) pursuant to Section 99 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume I and request for a certificate of destruction from the concerned COA Auditor in order to formally drop the destroyed accountable forms from the Monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms.

We recommended that the Management  do the following:
Instruct the Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer to submit the lacking   supporting   documents   as elaborated in item 2 of the post-audit conducted on the   liquidation of Social Pension to beneficiaries and ensure attachment of said document upon cash advance and liquidation. Likewise, ensure that the discrepancies noted in item 3 are addressed.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of supporting documents in all cash advances and payments such as the MOA and the certified list of eligible beneficiaries of Social Pension in compliance with Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 otherwise known as the Government Auditing Code of the Philippines.
Advise the Municipal Treasurer to assist and monitor the payments of social pension, together with the MSWDO in order to address the discrepancies noted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive thru the Solid Waste Management Board facilitate the speedy siting and establishment of a sanitary landfill in order to achieve an effective solid waste disposal system for the Municipality without prejudice to the administrative sanction provided in Section 50 of R.A. No. 9003.  Likewise, request the Local Council to augment the fund for sanitary landfill in compliance with the requirements of Sections 40 and 41 of RA 9003.

8. We recommended and Management  agreed  to  instruct  the  Municipal
        Accountant 	6 Designate to make the necessary adjusting  entries  to
        correct the erroneous  accounting  entries  in  the  GF  books  and
        establish the Trust Liability-DRRM in the TF books.  Further,  they
        also  agreed  to  follow  strictly  the  recording  and   reporting
        guidelines for LDRRMF as prescribed in COA Circular 2012-002  dated
        September 12, 2012.

        Below is the Audit Teams suggested accounting entry in CY 2018  TF
        books:

      Dr. Cash in Bank - LCCA           P 608,871.97
      Cr. Trust Liability-DRRM                     P 608,871.97

     9. As to prior years recording of the unexpended balance in GF books,
        they also agreed to direct the  Municipal  Accountant-Designate  to
        gather the original journal entry  vouchers  to  check  the  actual
        entries made and make the necessary adjusting  entries  to  correct
        the errors in the said books.


    10. Management committed strict adherence to the audit recommendations.

5. We recommended and Management agreed to strictly  adhere  with  the
        requirements and provisions of existing laws concerning  Accounting
        for Supplies and Materials and:


        a. instruct the Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of  using
           expense method in recording supplies  and  materials  purchases,
           maintain an inventory account using perpetual  inventory  system
           and prepare supplies ledger card per stock number;


        b. instruct  the  Municipal  Treasurer  to  maintain  supplies  and
           materials stock cards per stock number and  prepare  Summary  of
           Supplies and  Materials  Issued  and  submit  the  same  to  the
           Municipal Accountant for recording purposes  to  fairly  present
           its inventory accounts in the financial statements; and


        c. supervise the proper implementation  of  the  inventory  process
           regularly to ensure its effectiveness.


     6. Management committed strict adherence to the audit recommendations.

12. We recommended and Management agreed to  instruct  their  Inventory
        Committee to conduct annual physical count of all properties  owned
        by the Municipality and prepare a report thereon to be submitted to
        COA not later than January 31 of each  year.  Moreover,  they  also
        agreed to direct the Treasury Office and the Accounting  Office  to
        prepare  and  maintain  Property  Cards  and  PPE  Ledgers   Cards,
        respectively and ensure that reconciliation  of  their  records  is
        done before preparation of the annual financial statements.


    13. Management committed strict adherence to the audit recommendations.

We have recommended that management strictly implement our previous year audit recommendations in the conduct of actual physical inventory of the Property, Plant and Equipment, to wit:

a.Issue an order/memorandum for the conduct of physical inventory taking and be completed within a determined period of time. 

Require the inventory committee to prepare and submit the Report of Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) and Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) within ten (10) days from completion of the inventory as required under Section 124 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System, Vol. I. 

A complete  Property,  Plant  and Equipment and Supplies Ledger Cards be maintained by the Accounting Office to support the details and breakdown. In the same manner, the GSO also maintain Property and Stock Cards to all pertinent information of deliveries and acceptance of fixed assets/supplies. The accounting records be reconciled against the  property records.

 Extra effort be exerted by both the Accounting and General Services Office to determine the details of the said fixed asset accounts. 

 Update/renew Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) on equipment/properties issued to various municipal officials and employees.

All properties of the municipality must have a property tag or sticker pasted on each item for proper identification.

Drop the unserviceable properties in the books totalling P2,379,793.10  to ensure the correctness and accuracy of the Property, Plant and Equipment  accounts.
observations.reason: This was reiterated in Observation No. 1 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare the Certified List of Real Property Taxpayers with the corresponding amount of RPT/SET due and collectible each year as basis in setting up the correct RPT/SET Receivables by the Municipal Accountant. The Municipal Accountant make proper coordination with the Municipal Treasurer and secure copies of the accomplished identified taxpayer cards as basis in posting the RPT/SET Receivable accounts to ensure that the respective accounts are accurately presented in the financial statements. Further, the Municipal Accountant make necessary adjustments to arrive at a reliable and correct balance of the accounts.
observations.reason: The preparation of a certified list of real property taxpayers with the corresponding amount due and demandable is still on-going.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Accounting Office look into the balances of the said accounts and reconcile the same for proper presentation in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Accounting and Treasury Offices trace the  discrepancies noted and reconcile their records immediately.  Any discrepancy noted be verified and adjusted so that the cashbook balances of the cash in bank are reconciled with the ledger balances. Moreover, prepare reconciliation more often to avoid accumulation of differences at year end.

We have recommended  that the Municipal Treasurer secure an authority from the Department of Finance to maintain deposits with the Philippine Veterans Bank in order to ensure that government funds are adequately safeguarded and duly protected in case of bankruptcy.
observations.managementAction: No action
observations.reason: The Municipality did not act on this observation because of the latest circular issued by DOF.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the BAC strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations as regards the prohibition of using brand name in its procurement. The BAC use technical specifications, relevant characteristics and performance requirement of the item to be procured rather than by its name.

We have recommended that Management adhere with the Annual Procurement Plan in the identification of the more suitable method of procurement to be used in the procurement process. We have further recommended that the BAC Secretariat consolidate PPMPs from various units of the procuring entity to make them available for review as indicated in Section 7 of the IRR of RA 9184, prepare the Annual Procurement Plan and copy furnish the Audit Team for reference.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management utilize the 20% Development Fund during the year to address the priority needs of the people and to attain maximum benefits for the intended recipients.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management ensure the submission of the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and RA 10121 in order to evaluate whether the utilization of the fund is within the identified programs, projects and activities in the plan.

We have recommended that the Local Finance Committee and the Municipal School Board stop the utilization of the SEF to programs and projects not included in the provisions under RA 7160 and the new DECS, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01 s. 2017 dated January 19, 2017 in order to maximize the benefits that the students and teachers could avail under this fund.
We have recommended that the prescribed minimum employee’s monthly net take home pay under General Appropriations Act (GAA)  be observed.
observations.reason: A review of the net take home pay of each employee was started  by the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management refrain from procuring accountable forms from private entity without first securing the services of recognized government printers as required under Section 23 of the General Appropriations Act 2016.

We have recommended that agency officials concerned should require contractors to post  warranty security, one year after completion of the project, for the protection of the interest of the municipality in case of  structural defects and failures that may arise. The amount shall be released only after the lapse of the applicable warranty periods pursuant to the above regulations. Likewise, the Municipal Engineer being incharge in the implementation of  infrastructure projects of the municipality should monitor compliance by contractors of this requirements to avoid suspensions/disallowances in audit.
We have recommended that the Agency should furnish the Auditor a copy of contract and its supporting documents within five (5) days upon execution  for review.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the contract of lease between the market stallholders and the municipality be revised to make more binding and forceful. Further, comply with the terms and conditions specified in the lease contract as basis in the collection and termination of the contract if there are violations.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that all disbursement vouchers are supported with complete documents before payment as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to avoid suspension in audit.
